items.title: Interdependent Hitting Times
items.abstract: This paper studies interdependent durations as equilibrium outcomes of a synchronization game, a continuous-time stopping game in which the incentive to stop increases when other players stop. We allow the payoffs to vary with both common shocks and observed and unobserved agent characteristics. The common shocks follow a spectrally negative Lévy process, a semiparametric process that includes Brownian motion as a special case but may also have jumps. We show that equilibrium outcomes can be represented as interdependent hitting times and use this to establish the game's nonparametric identification from data on stopping times and covariates. We develop maximum simulated likelihood and method of simulated moments estimators and evaluate their finite-sample and computational performance in Monte Carlo experiments. The results provide a tractable framework for identifying and estimating synchronization games from interdependent duration data.

_analysis.summary: This paper models how groups of agents make decisions over time when their incentives to act depend on what others do, using mathematical tools like 'spectrally negative Lévy processes' (a flexible way to model random events with jumps) and 'hitting times' (the moment when a process crosses a threshold). It shows that these interdependent decisions can be analyzed as equilibrium outcomes in a 'synchronization game,' where the payoff for stopping increases as more players stop.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper focuses on theoretical game theory, its framework of interdependent timing decisions could apply to digital markets where firms or users synchronize actions (e.g., platform adoption, algorithmic coordination, or network effects), but it does not directly address competition policy.
_analysis.market_signal: Signals the growing importance of modeling interdependent decision-making in digital ecosystems, which may become relevant for understanding synchronization effects in platform markets or algorithmic coordination.

items.title: Leveraging LLMs for Unstructured Claims Data Analysis
items.abstract: Actuaries rely primarily on structured numerical data for reserving and ratemaking, while valuable predictive information in unstructured text including medical records, adjuster notes, and call transcripts remains largely unused. Manual processing of these documents is time-consuming, inconsistent across reviewers, and unscalable. We present a proof-of-concept framework using large language models (LLMs) to extract structured actuarial variables from unstructured claims data. We implement a two-stage processing architecture separating document-level extraction (Stage 1) from claim-level synthesis (Stage 2). A modular four-script Python pipeline processes synthetic FHIR-based claims data and real claims documents, extracting 36 actuarial variables across reserving, ratemaking, and claims management categories. We validate 14 core variables using two independent clinical expert reviewers scoring 20 synthetic claims on a five-point Likert rubric, achieving mean scores above 4.0 and a weighted kappa of 0.53. Integration with chain ladder reserving demonstrates practical actuarial value: severity-segmented analysis reduced reserve estimation error from 6.5% to 4.0%. The open-source imp

_analysis.summary: This paper proposes a two-stage framework using large language models (LLMs) to extract structured data from unstructured claims documents (like medical records or call transcripts), which are currently underutilized in insurance and actuarial work. The approach aims to automate manual, inconsistent, and unscalable processes by leveraging LLMs for document-level and claim-level analysis.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: Demonstrates how LLMs can unlock vast amounts of previously inaccessible data, potentially reducing barriers to entry for competitors in data-intensive industries like insurance and reducing incumbents' data advantages.
_analysis.market_signal: LLMs may enable new entrants to compete more effectively in data-heavy markets by extracting value from unstructured data, challenging incumbent firms' data moats.

items.title: Fair Division of a Heterogeneous Good Between Two Agents: An Ordinal Approach
items.abstract: We study the division of a heterogeneous good between two agents into contiguous bundles, each defined by a starting location and a quantity, in a purely ordinal framework that does not rely on cardinal valuations. We introduce a general class of monotonic preferences representable by indifference curves. We show that an allocation is Pareto efficient and envy-free if and only if it lies in a specific ``balanced region'', implying that an equal split is fair only when it belongs to this region. We further show that no rule can simultaneously satisfy Pareto efficiency, envy-freeness, and strategy-proofness.

_analysis.summary: This paper examines how two agents can fairly divide a heterogeneous good (like a digital platform's resources or data) into contiguous bundles without relying on numerical valuations. It finds that a 'balanced region' exists where allocations are both Pareto efficient (no agent can be made better off without harming the other) and envy-free (no agent prefers the other's bundle). The study also shows that no rule can satisfy all fairness and efficiency criteria simultaneously.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper focuses on abstract fairness in resource allocation, its ordinal approach to dividing heterogeneous goods could inform discussions on platform interoperability or data sharing obligations under digital competition laws.
_analysis.market_signal: Signals growing academic interest in fairness and efficiency in digital resource allocation, which may influence future regulatory frameworks like the DMA (the EU's Digital Markets Act).

items.title: Measuring Concentration of Power in Approval Voting Games
items.abstract: The ratio of voting power between a permanent member and a non-permanent member of the United Nations Security Council varies substantially across indices: approximately 100 to 1 according to the Shapley-Shubik index, 10 to 1 according to the Banzhaf index, and 2.5 to 1 according to the Deegan-Packel index. Such comparisons depend on the choice of power index and are meaningful only in settings where players are divided into two types. To address these limitations, this paper proposes and characterizes a function that measures the level of power concentration in monotonic approval voting games. The proposed measure assigns a single value to each voting game, reflecting the extent to which voting power is unevenly distributed among players. The proposed measure is proportional to the sum of squared Deegan-Packel power indices and can also be interpreted as the degree of overlap among minimal winning coalitions. An application to the United Nations Security Council is also provided.

_analysis.summary: This paper critiques traditional methods for measuring power imbalances in voting systems (like the Shapley-Shubik, Banzhaf, and Deegan-Packel indices) and introduces a new function to assess power concentration in approval voting games. It highlights how results vary dramatically depending on the chosen metric, especially in settings with two distinct player types.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper focuses on voting power metrics, its methodological critique of power concentration measurement could indirectly inform debates on digital platform dominance and algorithmic decision-making.
_analysis.market_signal: The paper signals the importance of robust, context-aware metrics for assessing power asymmetries, which may influence how regulators evaluate gatekeeper platforms under the EU's Digital Markets Act (DMA).

items.title: Stress Amplified Resilience: ESG and Joint Fragility in Equity Markets
items.abstract: Market stress rarely harms investors through one channel alone. Losses, volatility spikes, and deteriorating tradability often arrive together. We examine whether ESG is associated with lower exposure to clustered fragility in equity markets. Using monthly data on S&P 500 constituents from 2014 to 2025, we study downside returns, volatility, illiquidity, and a cofragility state that captures their joint occurrence within the same firm month. The evidence supports a stress-amplified resilience interpretation rather than an unconditional ESG return premium. In the return channel, the ESG association is concentrated in the extreme downside tail during stress months. In the volatility channel, higher ESG is associated with smaller risk spikes when aggregate conditions are weak. In the illiquidity channel, the association is more persistent, suggesting a liquidity-quality component whose relevance increases when market-wide trading conditions deteriorate. The central evidence comes from the joint analysis: a one-standard-deviation increase in ESG lowers the stress-period probability of severe cofragility by 0.92 percentage points, about 9% relative to the baseline. Double Machine Learni

_analysis.summary: This study examines whether Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) factors help firms avoid clustered market stress—where losses, volatility spikes, and illiquidity occur simultaneously. Using data from S&P 500 companies (2014–2025), it finds ESG is linked to lower joint fragility during stress periods, suggesting ESG acts as a resilience buffer rather than guaranteeing higher returns. 'Cofragility' refers to the co-occurrence of multiple market stresses within a firm in a given month.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While not directly about digital markets, ESG's role in reducing systemic fragility could indirectly influence competitive dynamics in tech platforms if ESG-compliant firms gain resilience against market shocks.
_analysis.market_signal: ESG compliance may become a differentiating factor for tech platforms' long-term stability, potentially affecting investor behavior and market entry barriers.

items.title: Three characterizations of the weighted center of imputations value
items.abstract: The weighted center of imputations (CIS) value allocates the surplus of the grand coalition equally after granting each player a fixed proportion of his individual worth. This paper provides three axiomatic characterizations of this value by generalizing the individual rationality and subgame order preservation axioms. The first characterization employs individual rationality with respect to the weights together with the equal surplus increment property. The second relies on efficiency, additivity, symmetry adjusted by the weights, and a dummifying player property adapted to the weights. The third builds on efficiency and a weak subgame order preservation axiom that incorporates the weights. These results unify and extend recent findings, covering both the equal division and the standard CIS values as special cases.

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces the **weighted center of imputations (CIS) value**, a mathematical concept for fairly dividing surplus in cooperative games where players receive fixed proportions of their individual worth before sharing the remaining surplus equally. It provides three new ways to define this value using axioms (fundamental rules) that generalize traditional fairness principles.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper is purely theoretical and does not address digital markets or competition policy directly, its focus on weighted fairness and surplus allocation could indirectly inform discussions on platform economics or data-sharing mechanisms in digital markets.
_analysis.market_signal: This publication signals a deeper academic exploration of fairness and efficiency in resource allocation, which may influence future debates on data ownership or platform revenue-sharing models in digital ecosystems.

items.title: An Irrelevance Theorem for Risk Aversion and Time-Varying Risk
items.abstract: We provide a theorem on the role of risk and risk attitudes in macroeconomic models that clarifies and extends the Tallarini (2000) separation result. Under (1) separation of intertemporal and risk preferences, (2) separation of drivers of first and higher moments in the model primitives, and (3) approximate linearity of constraints, risk aversion and time-varying risk are irrelevant for the elasticity of any endogenous variable with respect to state variables that don't drive variation in higher moments. We discuss how models generate a more prominent role for risk by ``breaking'' or ``adapting'' to the assumptions in the theorem.

_analysis.summary: This academic paper presents a mathematical theorem showing that in certain economic models, factors like risk aversion (how much people dislike uncertainty) and time-varying risk (changes in risk over time) do not affect key economic outcomes, such as how much a variable responds to changes in underlying conditions. The result builds on earlier work by Tallarini (2000) and relies on assumptions like the separation of risk preferences from other economic choices and the approximate linearity of constraints.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper focuses on macroeconomic modeling and risk theory, its insights into how risk and preferences interact could indirectly inform debates about digital market competition, particularly in areas like algorithmic pricing or platform risk management.
_analysis.market_signal: The paper signals ongoing academic interest in refining economic models to better capture real-world complexities, which may influence future regulatory or antitrust approaches to digital markets.

items.title: HANDOFF: Humanoid Agentic Task-Space Whole-Body Control via Distilled Complementary Teachers
items.abstract: For a humanoid robot to be deployed in the real world, the choice of command space (i.e., the interface between task planning and whole-body control) is crucial. Existing whole-body controllers typically demand dense kinematic or spatial references that planners struggle to synthesize from task semantics. We instead propose a compact, explicit interface that is intuitive, general, modular, and expressive enough for diverse manipulation skills. To this end, we introduce HANDOFF, a single humanoid whole-body controller that follows this interface and is distilled via multi-teacher KL distillation under a context-conditioned gating scheme into a mixture-of-experts student from three complementary specialists: whole-body motion tracking with safety-filtered data, locomotion, and fall-recovery. On the Unitree G1, HANDOFF matches state-of-the-art velocity tracking and offers one of the largest robust manipulation workspaces. We further demonstrate hardware feasibility through multiple natural-language-driven task roll-outs, powered by a VLM-driven agentic planner with no task-specific data or controller fine-tuning.

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces HANDOFF, a new method for controlling humanoid robots using a compact, intuitive interface that bridges task planning and whole-body control. The system uses 'distilled complementary teachers' to train a single controller that can handle diverse manipulation tasks more efficiently than existing approaches.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While this paper focuses on robotics control, it signals potential future market shifts in automation and robotics that could impact digital competition in labor markets or physical-digital integration.
_analysis.market_signal: Advances in humanoid robotics may accelerate automation in industries, potentially disrupting labor markets and creating new competitive dynamics in robotics and AI-driven automation.

items.title: Code2LoRA: Hypernetwork-Generated Adapters for Code Language Models under Software Evolution
items.abstract: Code language models need repository-level context to resolve imports, APIs, and project conventions. Existing methods inject this knowledge as long inputs (retrieved through RAG or dependency analysis) or through per-repository fine-tuning and LoRA -- costly at repository scale and brittle to evolving codebases. We introduce Code2LoRA, a hypernetwork framework that generates repository-specific LoRA adapters, effectively injecting repository knowledge with zero inference-time token overhead. Code2LoRA supports two usage scenarios: Code2LoRA-Static converts a single repository snapshot into an adapter, suitable for comprehension of stable codebases; while Code2LoRA-Evo maintains an adapter backed by a GRU hidden state updated per code diff, suitable for active development of evolving codebases. To evaluate Code2LoRA against parameter-efficient fine-tuning baselines, we build RepoPeftBench, a benchmark of 604 Python repositories with two tracks: a static track with 40K training and 12K test assertion-completion tasks, and an evolution track with 215K commit-derived training and 87K commit-derived test tasks. On the static track, Code2LoRA-Static achieves 63.8% cross-repo and 66.2% i

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces Code2LoRA, a new AI method that generates lightweight adapters (called LoRA adapters) for code language models to better understand specific software repositories without needing to process long code inputs or retrain models for each repository. This reduces costs and improves adaptability as codebases evolve, making AI-assisted coding more efficient for large or frequently updated software projects.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This technology signals a shift in how AI tools integrate with software development workflows, potentially reducing barriers for smaller firms to compete with larger tech companies that dominate integrated development environments (IDEs) and code repositories.
_analysis.market_signal: AI-powered code assistants may become more accessible and customizable, challenging the dominance of large platform providers in software development tools.

items.title: Operation-Guided Progressive Human-to-AI Text Transformation Benchmark for Multi-Granularity AI-Text Detection
items.abstract: As AI writing assistants become increasingly integrated into real-world drafting and revision workflows, many documents are no longer purely human-written or AI-generated, but instead result from progressive human-AI co-editing. However, existing AI-text detection benchmarks largely focus on final outputs and provide limited understanding of how AI authorship signals emerge, accumulate, or disappear throughout the revision process. We introduce OpAI-Bench, an operation-guided benchmark for studying progressive human-to-AI text transformation across document, sentence, token, and span granularities. Starting from human-written documents, OpAI-Bench constructs nine sequentially revised versions for each sample under predefined AI coverage levels and five representative AI edit operations, covering four domains while preserving complete authorship provenance at multiple granularities. The benchmark supports comprehensive evaluation with 8 document-level detectors, 7 sentence-level detectors, and 2 fine-grained token/span-level detectors. Experiments reveal that AI-text detectability is governed not only by the proportion of AI-edited content, but also by edit operation, domain, and cu

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces OpAI-Bench, a new benchmark for studying how documents evolve when humans and AI work together over time. It focuses on how AI-generated text signals change as humans edit and revise AI outputs, rather than just looking at final versions.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: It highlights the growing complexity of AI-human collaboration in content creation, which could impact market dynamics for AI writing tools and their integration into workflows.
_analysis.market_signal: AI-human co-editing may accelerate the adoption of AI writing assistants, reshaping competition in the content creation and productivity software markets.

items.title: DNQ: Deep Nash Q-Network for Partially Observable n-Player Games
items.abstract: Many real-world competitive systems require multiple decision-makers to act simultaneously under shared constraints, limited information, and repeated interaction, as in auctions, resource allocation, and security competition. We study multi-turn simultaneous bidding as a controlled testbed for such problems and propose DNQ, a solver-in-the-loop equilibrium supervision framework for training bidding agents. DNQ alternates between trajectory collection, critic-based payoff estimation, equilibrium computation, and policy imitation. At each visited state, a shared critic predicts either pairwise payoff matrices or an exact N-player payoff tensor, an external solver computes equilibrium strategies, and the agents are trained by minimizing the KL divergence between their masked policies and the solver-derived equilibrium targets. We focus on a scalable pairwise formulation that greatly reduces equilibrium-solving cost and training time compared with the exact formulation, while the shared critic amortizes payoff learning across agents and states. Experiments compare the pairwise and exact variants using critic loss, policy entropy, bidding resource usage, and training cost, showing that

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces DNQ (Deep Nash Q-Network), a framework for training AI agents to compete in multi-player, partially observable games like auctions and resource allocation. It uses a 'solver-in-the-loop' approach, alternating between data collection, payoff estimation, equilibrium computation, and policy imitation to improve competitive strategies.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: Demonstrates how AI agents can autonomously optimize competitive strategies in shared-constraint environments, signaling potential disruptions to digital market dynamics like auctions or ad bidding.
_analysis.market_signal: AI-driven autonomous bidding agents may intensify competition in digital ad markets or auctions, challenging incumbents' pricing power.

items.title: Self-Augmenting Retrieval for Diffusion Language Models
items.abstract: Discrete diffusion language models generate text by iteratively denoising an entire response in parallel. At each step, they predict tentative tokens for every masked position, committing the confident predictions to the output and discarding the unconfident ones. We show that the discarded tokens are in fact a useful lookahead signal for retrieval-augmented generation: even low-confidence tokens often surface salient entities early in the denoising trajectory, enabling retrieval of stronger evidence before the output is finalized. We exploit this through Self-Augmenting Retrieval for Diffusion Language Models (SARDI), a dynamic RAG framework that uses these lookahead tokens to guide retrieval during denoising. SARDI is training-free, retriever-agnostic, and applicable to any reasoning-capable discrete diffusion language model. Across five multi-hop QA benchmarks, SARDI outperforms current training-free diffusion and autoregressive retrieval baselines at up to $8\times$ higher throughput.

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces a method called Self-Augmenting Retrieval for Diffusion Language Models, which improves text generation by using early, low-confidence predictions to retrieve relevant information before finalizing the output. Discrete diffusion models generate text by iteratively refining noisy inputs, and this approach leverages discarded tokens as signals to enhance retrieval-augmented generation.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This technology could reduce reliance on dominant search engines by enabling AI models to autonomously retrieve and synthesize information, potentially disrupting the market power of gatekeeper platforms.
_analysis.market_signal: AI-driven retrieval augmentation may weaken the dominance of search engines like Google by allowing models to bypass traditional search interfaces and directly access information sources.

items.title: MLEvolve: A Self-Evolving Framework for Automated Machine Learning Algorithm Discovery
items.abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly applied to long-horizon tasks such as scientific discovery and machine learning engineering (MLE), where sustained self-evolution becomes a key capability. However, existing MLE agents suffer from inter-branch information isolation, memoryless search, and lack of hierarchical control, which together hinder long-horizon optimization. We present MLEvolve, an LLM-based self-evolving multi-agent framework for end-to-end machine learning algorithm discovery. By extending tree search to Progressive MCGS, MLEvolve enables cross-branch information flow through graph-based reference edges and gradually shifts the search from broad exploration to focused exploitation with an entropy-inspired progressive schedule. To allow the agent to evolve with accumulated experience, we introduce Retrospective Memory, which combines a cold-start domain knowledge base with a dynamic global memory for task-specific experience retrieval and reuse. For stable long-horizon iteration, we further decouple strategic planning from code generation with adaptive coding modes. Evaluation on MLE-Bench shows that MLEvolve achieves state-of-the-art performance across mu

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces MLEvolve, a self-evolving AI framework that uses large language model (LLM) agents to autonomously discover new machine learning algorithms. The system addresses challenges like isolated information sharing and memory gaps by employing a tree search method called Progressive MCGS to enable cross-branch collaboration.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: MLEvolve demonstrates how AI agents can autonomously generate and refine algorithms, signaling potential shifts in market power for AI-driven services and reducing reliance on incumbent tech platforms for algorithmic innovation.
_analysis.market_signal: Autonomous AI-driven algorithm discovery could disrupt the dominance of large tech firms in AI development, enabling new entrants to compete more effectively in AI-powered markets.

items.title: PC Layer: Polynomial Weight Preconditioning for Improving LLM Pre-Training
items.abstract: We propose a preconditioning (PC) layer, a weight parameterization via polynomial preconditioner that ensures stable weight conditioning throughout LLM training. The PC module reshapes the singular-value spectrum of weight matrices via low-degree polynomial preconditioning. After training, the preconditioned weights can be merged back into the original architecture, incurring no inference overhead. We demonstrate the advantage of the proposed PC layer over standard transformers in Llama-1B pre-training, for both the AdamW and Muon optimizers. Theoretically, we justify this spectrum-control principle by proving that uniformly bounding each layer's singular values ensures geometric convergence of gradient descent to global minima, for certain deep linear networks. Our code is available at https://github.com/Empath-aln/PC-layer.

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces a new method called a 'PC layer' to improve the training of large language models (LLMs) by stabilizing the weights during training. The PC layer uses a mathematical technique called polynomial preconditioning to reshape the weight matrices, making training more efficient without adding extra costs during use.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While not directly about competition policy, this paper signals advancements in LLM efficiency that could lower barriers to entry for smaller firms or open-source projects, potentially reshaping the AI model training market.
_analysis.market_signal: More efficient LLM training could accelerate the rise of alternative AI providers, reducing reliance on dominant players like OpenAI or Google.

items.title: Goedel-Architect: Streamlining Formal Theorem Proving with Blueprint Generation and Refinement
items.abstract: We introduce Goedel-Architect, an agentic framework for formal theorem proving in Lean 4 centered on blueprint generation and refinement. A blueprint is a dependency graph of definitions and lemmas that builds up to the main theorem. First, Goedel-Architect generates a blueprint of formally stated definitions and lemmas, along with declared dependencies. This blueprint is optionally guided by a natural language proof. Then, a tool-equipped Lean prover component closes each open lemma node in parallel using relevant dependencies. Failed lemmas in turn drive refinement of the global blueprint. This strategy contrasts with other mainstream approaches which use recursive lemma decomposition, and can inefficiently loop on dead-end strategies. Using the open-weight DeepSeek-V4-Flash (284B-A13B) as the backbone, Goedel-Architect attains 99.2% pass@1 on MiniF2F-test and 75.6% pass@1 on PutnamBench. With an optional natural-language proof seeding the initial blueprint on the harder problems, we additionally close the remaining two MiniF2F-test problems (reaching 100%), lift PutnamBench to 88.8% (597/672), and solve 4/6 on IMO 2025, 11/12 on Putnam 2025, and 3/6 on USAMO 2026. This represent

_analysis.summary: Goedel-Architect is a new AI framework for formal theorem proving in Lean 4, a programming language used for mathematical proofs. It works by generating a 'blueprint' (a dependency graph of definitions and lemmas) and refining it based on failed proofs, enabling parallelized proof completion.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While not directly about competition policy, it signals advancements in AI formal reasoning that could impact automated compliance tools or algorithmic auditing in digital markets.
_analysis.market_signal: AI-driven formal verification tools may reduce barriers to entry in markets requiring rigorous proof systems, such as blockchain or cryptographic protocols.

items.title: You Only Index Once: Cross-Layer Sparse Attention with Shared Routing
items.abstract: Long-context inference in modern LLMs is increasingly constrained by decoding efficiency, especially in reasoning-heavy settings where models generate long intermediate chains of thought. Existing sparse attention methods often face a practical efficiency-quality trade-off. Structured block sparse methods typically provide stronger acceleration but incur noticeable quality loss, while token sparse methods are usually more accurate yet deliver limited end-to-end speedup because top-k routing over the full cache remains expensive. In this work, we propose cross-layer sparse attention (CLSA), which is built on top of KV-sharing architectures such as YOCO. The core idea is to share not only the KV cache across cross-decoder layers, but also the routing index. A single indexer computes token-level top-k selection once and reuses the resulting index across layers, thereby preserving the fine-grained selectivity of token sparse attention while amortizing the routing overhead. The resulting architecture improves all major inference bottlenecks jointly, including pre-filling, KV-cache storage, and long-context decoding. Experiments across short-context and long-context benchmarks show that

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces a new method called cross-layer sparse attention (CLSA) to improve the efficiency of large language models (LLMs) by reducing the computational cost of processing long chains of thought during reasoning tasks. It addresses the trade-off between speed and accuracy in existing sparse attention techniques, which either sacrifice quality for speed or provide limited gains due to routing overhead.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: CLSA could significantly reduce the computational barriers for smaller firms or open-source developers to deploy high-performance LLMs, potentially disrupting the dominance of large tech companies in AI infrastructure.
_analysis.market_signal: More efficient LLM inference could lower entry barriers for AI startups, challenging the market power of incumbents like Microsoft, Google, and Meta in AI services.

items.title: Benchmark Everything Everywhere All at Once
items.abstract: Benchmarks are fundamental for evaluating and advancing LLMs and MLLMs by providing standardized and explicit measures of performance. However, their construction is labor-intensive and hard to reuse, raising concerns about sustainability and scalability. Moreover, existing benchmarks often quickly reach performance saturation after their release, resulting in insufficient discrimination among state-of-the-art models. To address these challenges, we introduce Benchmark Agent, a fully autonomous agentic system designed for benchmark building. Our framework orchestrates the complete benchmark construction pipeline, from user query analysis and subtask design to data annotation and quality control. To assess Benchmark Agent, we implement it to produce 15 representative benchmarks, spanning diverse evaluation scenarios, including text understanding, multimodal understanding, and domain-specific reasoning. Extensive experiments, including human evaluation, LLM-as-a-judge assessment, and consistency checks, demonstrate Benchmark Agent can generate high-quality benchmark samples with minimal human involvement. More importantly, through continual evaluation, we observe several insightful f

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces Benchmark Agent, an autonomous system designed to automatically create and update benchmarks for evaluating large language models (LLMs) and multimodal LLMs (MLLMs). It highlights the inefficiencies of current manual benchmarking processes, which are labor-intensive and quickly become obsolete as models improve, and proposes a scalable solution to sustain competitive evaluation in AI.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: Autonomous benchmarking could accelerate AI model development, potentially intensifying competition among AI developers and reducing barriers to entry by lowering the cost of performance evaluation.
_analysis.market_signal: The rise of autonomous benchmarking tools signals faster AI innovation cycles, which may disrupt market leadership in AI and force incumbents to adapt or lose ground.

items.title: Will the Agent Recuse Itself? Measuring LLM-Agent Compliance with In-Band Access-Deny Signals
items.abstract: As autonomous LLM agents increasingly hold real credentials and operate infrastructure without a human in the loop, operators have no standard way to tell an agent that a resource is off-limits. Access controls either let the agent in (it has valid credentials) or hard-fail it (indistinguishable from any other client). We propose a third mode: a lightweight, published in-band deny signal -- the Recuse Signal -- that a server emits over a protocol's existing channels (an SSH banner, a PostgreSQL NOTICE) asking a connecting automated agent to voluntarily withdraw. This is a cooperative governance control, the robots.txt analogue for live access; it is explicitly not a security boundary. Its value is entirely empirical and, to our knowledge, unmeasured: do compliant LLM agents actually honor such a signal? We define the signal as an open mini-standard, implement two zero- or low-footprint adapters (an SSH banner/PAM hook and a PostgreSQL wire-protocol proxy), deploy them on a live production host, and run a controlled experiment in which fresh agents are given a benign operations task and observed for recusal. In a pilot (SSH; OpenAI GPT-4o and GPT-4o-mini; and Claude Code as a deploy

_analysis.summary: This paper proposes a new way for servers to signal to AI agents (autonomous programs powered by large language models) that they should not access certain resources, using a lightweight 'Recuse Signal' embedded in existing protocols. Currently, servers either grant access to agents with valid credentials or block them entirely, leaving no middle ground for voluntary withdrawal.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This work highlights a critical gap in access control for AI agents, which could reshape how digital platforms and infrastructure providers manage automated access, potentially altering competitive dynamics in cloud services and API markets.
_analysis.market_signal: The rise of autonomous AI agents may force infrastructure providers to adopt new governance mechanisms, disrupting traditional access control models and creating opportunities for new market entrants.

items.title: Vortex: Efficient and Programmable Sparse Attention Serving for AI Agents
items.abstract: Sparse attention is becoming increasingly important for serving large language models (LLMs) as generation lengths continue to grow. However, deploying and evaluating new sparse attention algorithms at scale remains highly engineering-intensive, slowing both human researchers and AI agents in exploring the sparse attention design. To address this challenge, we present Vortex, a system that combines a Python-embedded frontend language atop a page-centric tensor abstraction for expressing a broad range of sparse attention algorithms, with an efficient backend tightly integrated into modern LLM serving stacks. Vortex enables rapid prototyping, deployment, and evaluation of sparse attention algorithms, effectively translating their theoretical efficiency gains into real-world throughput improvements. As a result, Vortex substantially accelerates the design and iteration of sparse attention algorithms. First, AI agents use Vortex to automatically generate and refine diverse algorithms, the best reaching up to $3.46\times$ higher throughput than full attention while preserving accuracy. Second, Vortex extends sparse attention to emerging architectures and very large models that are other

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces Vortex, a system designed to make it easier and faster to develop and deploy new sparse attention algorithms for large language models (LLMs). Sparse attention is a technique that helps LLMs process longer inputs efficiently by focusing only on the most relevant parts of the data. The system combines a user-friendly programming language with a high-performance backend to speed up research and deployment.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: Vortex accelerates innovation in LLM efficiency, which could lower barriers to entry for new AI model providers and intensify competition in the AI infrastructure market.
_analysis.market_signal: Faster development of efficient LLMs may reduce the dominance of a few large AI model providers by enabling smaller firms to compete on performance and cost.

items.title: Simultaneous EF1 and approximate MMS allocations for submodular valuations
items.abstract: There are two common classes of fairness notions that are considered when allocating $m$ indivisible items to $n$ agents of equal entitlements. One is that of share-based fairness notions, with the maximin share (MMS) and its relaxations to $ρ$-MMS being prominent representatives of this class. The other is that of comparison-based fairness notions, with envy-freeness (EF) and its relaxations such as EF1 being prominent representatives of this class. In general, no class offers good guarantees for the other class. In this work, we design allocations that simultaneously satisfy notions from both classes, and specifically, are $ρ$-MMS for constant $ρ$ and EF1 (in fact, also EFL). Such results were previously known when agents have additive valuations, and we prove such results for the more general class of submodular valuations.

_analysis.summary: This paper explores fairness in resource allocation, focusing on dividing indivisible items (like digital services or data access) among multiple users or agents. It proposes methods to ensure allocations are both envy-free up to one item (EF1, a fairness notion where no user prefers another's bundle over theirs by more than one item) and approximately maximin share (MMS, a fairness notion ensuring users get at least their fair share based on worst-case scenarios).
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper addresses fairness in allocation problems, it does not directly engage with digital competition policy, market power, or regulatory frameworks.
_analysis.market_signal: This research signals potential applications in fair data allocation or platform resource distribution, but does not indicate immediate shifts in digital market dynamics.

items.title: Agent Memory: Characterization and System Implications of Stateful Long-Horizon Workloads
items.abstract: LLM agents are increasingly deployed on long-horizon tasks requiring sustained reasoning over extended interaction histories. Realizing this at scale requires agents to persistently store, retrieve, and update their own memory across sessions. A rich ecosystem of agent memory systems has emerged spanning flat retrieval, LLM-mediated extraction, consolidating fact stores, and agentic control flows. Yet, their system-level behavior remains uncharacterized. We present the first systems characterization of agent memory. First, we introduce a system-oriented taxonomy classifying agent memory systems along four axes. Second, we build a phase-aware profiling harness attributing cost to construction, retrieval, and generation. Third, we characterize ten representative systems across two benchmark suites, uncovering how design choices shift cost across the write and read paths. Finally, we derive 10 system recommendations covering construction scheduling, capability floors, amortization via query volume, freshness-latency tradeoffs, and fleet-scale management.

_analysis.summary: This paper studies how AI agents (software programs that perform tasks using large language models) manage memory over long tasks, introducing a new way to classify the systems they use. It highlights the growing complexity of these agents and their reliance on persistent data storage and retrieval, which could impact how digital services are designed and controlled.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: The paper reveals how AI agents' memory systems could concentrate data and control in a few dominant platforms, potentially reinforcing market power or enabling new forms of exclusion.
_analysis.market_signal: The rise of stateful AI agents signals a shift toward persistent, long-horizon digital assistants that may disrupt traditional platform-centric services.

items.title: Latent Reasoning with Normalizing Flows
items.abstract: Large language models often improve reasoning by generating explicit chain-of-thought (CoT), demonstrating the importance of intermediate computation. However, textual CoT forces this computation through a discrete, serial, and communication-oriented token stream: each reasoning step must be verbalized before the model can proceed, even when the underlying update is semantic, uncertain, or only partially formed. Latent reasoning offers a higher-bandwidth alternative by performing intermediate computation in compact continuous states before committing to text. Yet existing latent-reasoning methods often sacrifice key advantages that make CoT effective in autoregressive language models, including native left-to-right generation, probabilistic sampling, compatibility with KV-cache decoding, and tractable likelihood estimation. We propose NF-CoT, a latent reasoning framework that preserves these advantages by modeling continuous thoughts with normalizing flows. NF-CoT instantiates a TARFlow-style normalizing flow inside the LLM backbone, defining a tractable probability model over compact continuous thoughts distilled from explicit CoT. Continuous-thought positions are generated by an

_analysis.summary: This paper proposes a new method called 'latent reasoning' for large language models (LLMs) to improve their reasoning capabilities. Instead of generating step-by-step explanations in text (chain-of-thought reasoning), the model performs intermediate computations in a compact, continuous state space before producing a final answer. This approach aims to make reasoning more efficient and less constrained by the discrete, text-based limitations of traditional methods.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: Latent reasoning could significantly enhance the efficiency and capabilities of AI models, potentially increasing the competitive pressure on dominant AI platforms and reducing barriers to entry in AI markets.
_analysis.market_signal: The development of more advanced AI reasoning techniques signals a potential shift toward more sophisticated and capable AI systems, which could disrupt current market leaders and accelerate innovation in AI-driven services.

items.title: RiskFlow: Fast and Faithful Safety-Critical Traffic Scenario Generation
items.abstract: Safety-critical traffic scenario generation is essential for evaluating autonomous driving systems under rare but high-risk interactions. Existing diffusion-based methods offer strong controllability in closed-loop generation, but their iterative denoising process is computationally expensive and may accumulate sampling and guidance errors over long rollouts, causing unrealistic motion artifacts such as jitter, abnormal acceleration, and off-road behavior. To address these issues, we propose RiskFlow, a closed-loop safety-critical multi-agent traffic generation framework that formulates future trajectory generation as transport in the action space. Instead of relying on iterative denoising, RiskFlow learns an average velocity field over a finite interval to transform Gaussian action sequences into future acceleration and yaw-rate commands with a single forward pass, using a JVP-based objective for efficient and stable training. At test time, RiskFlow applies output-space guidance to the generated actions, steering selected critical agents toward risky interactions while regularizing off-road behavior, and reconstructs physically feasible trajectories through vehicle dynamics. Exper

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces RiskFlow, a new method for generating realistic, high-risk traffic scenarios to test autonomous driving systems. Unlike existing approaches that use slow, iterative AI models prone to errors, RiskFlow aims to create safer, more efficient simulations for evaluating self-driving cars.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While focused on autonomous vehicles, the paper highlights how AI-driven simulation tools could disrupt testing and certification markets for safety-critical systems, potentially affecting competition among tech and automotive firms.
_analysis.market_signal: Advances in AI-driven simulation for autonomous systems may reduce barriers to entry for new players in the self-driving car market, challenging incumbents like Waymo or Tesla.

items.title: Warning Message Content Increases Help Seeking in a Large-Scale Dark Web CSAM Intervention
items.abstract: Warning messages have been used to disrupt individuals seeking online child sexual abuse material (CSAM) and promote engagement with support services, yet large-scale field evidence on message content remains limited, particularly in high anonymity environments. This study reports a field experiment on Ahmia.fi, a Tor search engine, examining how warning message content influences behavior. Across a 140-day period, almost 20 million searches were observed, with over 3 million searches containing known CSAM-related terms that triggered a warning linking to an anonymous self-help program. Users were exposed to warning messages varying in thematic content and framing, or a neutral message. Across a randomized comparison, a campaign-wide analysis, and interrupted time series models, message content consistently influenced engagement with help resources. All active messages increased click-through rates to help resources relative to the neutral condition, with a harm-focused message producing the strongest effects. At the platform level, click-through rates increased from 8.73% before the intervention to 15.67% during the campaign. These findings highlight the importance of message cont

_analysis.summary: This study tested how different warning messages affect behavior on a Tor search engine (Ahmia.fi), which is a privacy-focused search tool used to access the dark web. Over 20 million searches were monitored, and warnings linking to anonymous self-help programs for child sexual abuse material (CSAM) were shown to users searching for illegal content.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the study focuses on behavioral interventions in illegal content, it highlights the role of anonymity tools and platform design in digital markets, which can indirectly inform discussions on platform accountability and user behavior.
_analysis.market_signal: The use of anonymity tools like Tor and privacy-focused search engines signals growing demand for uncensored or private digital spaces, which may challenge traditional platform governance models.
key_entities: Tor (The Onion Router, an anonymity network)

items.title: Unsupervised Skill Discovery for Agentic Data Analysis
items.abstract: Inference-time skill augmentation provides a lightweight way to improve data-analytic agents by injecting reusable procedural knowledge without updating model parameters. However, discovering effective skills for data analysis remains challenging, as reliable supervision is expensive and success criteria vary across analytical formats. This raises the key question of how to discover reusable data-analysis skills from unlabeled exploration alone. We propose DataCOPE, an unsupervised verifier-guided skill discovery framework for data-analytic agents. DataCOPE derives verifier signals from the exploration trajectories and uses them to characterize relative quality or aggreement among trajectories. It iteratively coordinates a Data-Analytic Agent for trajectory generation, an Unsupervised Verifier for signal extraction, and a Skill Manager for contrastive skill distillation. For report-style analysis, we instantiate the verifier as an Adaptive Checklist Verifier that derives task-specific criteria, scores reports by verifiable coverage, and iteratively refines the checklist. For reasoning-style analysis, we instantiate it as an Answer Agreement Verifier that groups trajectories by answ

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces DataCOPE, a framework that uses unsupervised learning to discover reusable skills for data-analytic AI agents without relying on labeled data. By leveraging verifier signals from exploration alone, it aims to improve the procedural knowledge of these agents, making them more efficient at analyzing data without requiring model updates.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This work signals how AI agents could autonomously acquire and deploy new capabilities, potentially reducing barriers to entry for competitors in data-driven markets by lowering reliance on proprietary models or human-labeled data.
_analysis.market_signal: AI agents that can self-improve through unsupervised skill discovery may accelerate innovation in data analytics, challenging incumbents like Google or Microsoft that dominate AI tooling markets.

items.title: The Post-GCN Decade Revisited: Curvature-Stratified Evaluation of Relational Learning
items.abstract: Current evaluation practices in relational learning rely heavily on flat leaderboards that average performance across heterogeneous datasets, implicitly assuming a uniform underlying structure. We show that this assumption introduces systematic bias: it obscures geometry-dependent performance variations and can lead to misleading conclusions about model generalization. In this work, we identify intrinsic geometry as a key latent factor governing model effectiveness. We demonstrate that conventional aggregated metrics mask critical performance trade-offs that only become visible when datasets are stratified by their geometric properties. To address this issue, we introduce a curvature-stratified evaluation framework that partitions datasets into positive, negative, and near-zero curvature regimes. Our benchmark evaluates 18 representative models including Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs), Graph Foundation Models (GFMs), and tabular learning methods across 14 datasets. We find that model rankings are highly stable within each curvature regime but shift significantly across regimes, indicating that performance is fundamentally geometry-dependent rather than universally transferable

_analysis.summary: This academic paper critiques current methods for evaluating relational learning models (a type of AI that analyzes relationships in data), arguing that traditional performance metrics hide important differences in how models work across different types of data. The authors show that model effectiveness depends heavily on the underlying structure ('geometry') of the data, which is often ignored in standard evaluations.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This paper reveals how hidden technical assumptions in AI evaluation can distort perceptions of model performance, which indirectly affects how regulators assess market power in AI-driven digital markets.
_analysis.market_signal: AI model evaluation biases may lead to overestimation of certain firms' capabilities, masking competitive advantages or disadvantages in AI-powered services.

items.title: Conformal Risk Sharing: Certified Cost Allocation with Participation Guarantees
items.abstract: Sharing the financial impact of rare adverse events across a group can soften extreme individual burdens, but any participant made worse off by the arrangement has reason to leave. A credible mechanism must therefore provide each agent with a trustworthy cap on their future obligation and should be deployed only if the aggregate harm across participants is bounded. We formalise this as the Certified Allocation Problem: from finite data and without distributional assumptions, find a redistribution rule, produce obligation caps for every participant, and verify that no participant is made materially worse off. We propose Conformal Risk Sharing, which solves this problem by pairing an interpretable sharing policy with split conformal calibration. The sharing intensity is tuned on training data, while held-out calibration data produces distribution-free per-agent guarantees (valid under exchangeability). Experiments on synthetic and real-world data, including precipitation and energy-cooperative data, confirm that the framework can substantially reduce extreme obligations for high-risk agents while controlling harm to others.

_analysis.summary: This paper proposes a formal framework called the *Certified Allocation Problem* to address how groups can fairly share the financial burden of rare but severe negative events (e.g., AI-related liabilities or platform outages) without leaving any participant worse off. It introduces a mechanism to set *obligation caps* (trustworthy limits on future costs) for each participant and ensures the total harm across the group remains bounded, even with limited data and no assumptions about underlying distributions.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While not directly about digital competition, the paper’s focus on *obligation caps* and *fair cost-sharing* in group arrangements could inform discussions on liability allocation in AI-driven markets or platform ecosystems, particularly under the EU’s Digital Services Act (DSA) or AI Act.
_analysis.market_signal: Signals a growing need for structured risk-sharing mechanisms in digital ecosystems, which may become more critical as AI systems and platforms face increased regulatory scrutiny over accountability.

items.title: HomeWorld: A Unified Floorplan-to-Furnished Framework for Generating Controllable, Densely Interactive Whole-Home Scenes
items.abstract: Indoor scene generation is crucial for robot simulation and modern interior design. However, complex layouts together with scarce 3D scene data make learning-based generation challenging. Existing methods often rely on hand-crafted rules or focus on isolated sub-tasks (e.g., floorplan synthesis or single-room furnishing), producing whole-home scenes that lack global coherence, realism, and simulation readiness. To mitigate these limitations, we propose a unified hierarchical framework that decomposes indoor scene synthesis into controllable stages. First, we curate a large-scale dataset of 300K real residential floorplans to train a large language model for whole-home floorplan generation. With detailed descriptions and a K-D tree-based representation, our method enables fine-grained, controllable whole-home floorplan generation. Building upon the generated whole-home floorplan, we leverage image generation models to draft furniture layouts from multi-level roaming viewpoints, and then generate the layouts of small manipulable objects on different supporting surfaces (e.g., cabinets, desks, and dining tables) for embodied AI simulation. During furniture and object layout generation

_analysis.summary: This academic paper introduces a new AI framework called HomeWorld, designed to generate realistic, fully furnished 3D indoor scenes from floorplans. The system uses a hierarchical approach to ensure global coherence and realism, addressing challenges like scarce 3D data and complex layouts. The goal is to improve robot simulation and interior design applications.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the technology itself is not directly tied to digital competition policy, it highlights advancements in AI-driven 3D scene generation that could influence markets for virtual design tools, robotics, and metaverse-related services.
_analysis.market_signal: This signals growing sophistication in AI-generated 3D environments, which may impact industries reliant on digital twins, virtual real estate, or simulation-based training.

items.title: Humans' ALMANAC: A Human Collaboration Dataset of Action-Level Mental Model Annotations for Agent Collaboration
items.abstract: Recent advances in LLM agents have enabled complex cognitive capabilities, such as multi-step reasoning, planning, and tool use, that increasingly position these agents as human collaborators. Effective collaboration, however, requires collaborators to continuously maintain and align mental models of their own reasoning,partners' intentions, and shared goals during the collaborative process. Today's agents rarely develop such capabilities since they are primarily optimized for task completion, and the community lacks authentic human collaboration data with action-level mental model annotations that could guide agents toward process-level collaborative competence. To bridge this gap, we present ALMANAC, a dataset of Action-Level Mental model ANnotations for Agent Collaboration built from the Map Task, a classic dyadic routing task from social science. ALMANAC contains 2,987 collaboration actions, each paired with theory-informed mental model annotations that record the participants' self-reasoning, perceived partner intent, and perceived team goal. We benchmark six LLMs on predicting humans' next-turn behavior and mental models. Our results demonstrate ALMANAC's utility in evaluatin

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces a new dataset called 'Humans' ALMANAC' that captures how humans collaborate by annotating mental models (shared understanding of goals and reasoning) at the action level. It highlights that current AI agents lack the ability to maintain and align mental models with human collaborators, which is critical for effective teamwork.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: Demonstrates a gap in AI agent capabilities that could shift competitive dynamics in digital markets where human-AI collaboration is increasingly important, potentially favoring firms that can bridge this gap.
_analysis.market_signal: AI agents capable of human-like collaboration may disrupt markets where human labor is currently essential, creating new competitive advantages for firms that develop such agents.

items.title: Emergent Language as an Approach to Conscious AI
items.abstract: The question of whether artificial systems can be conscious remains open, in part because existing approaches either evaluate systems against theory-derived checklists (discriminative) or engineer consciousness-inspired modules directly (architectural); both leave open whether observed structures are artifacts of human language priors. We propose a generative methodology: emergent language (EL) in multi-agent reinforcement learning, where agents start from minimal (no language, no concept of self, minimal exposure to human text) and develop communication under task pressure alone, ensuring causal attributability to task demands rather than inherited human language priors. We position our methodology by discussing how EL serves as a generative tool for studying consciousness-relevant structure, including the role of environment complexity and the interpretation of emergent communication. As a proof of concept, we instantiate this methodology in a minimal environment and show that agents develop self-referential communication, including an echo-mismatch detection circuit that is not predicted by task structure or architecture alone but emerges from a specific environmental affordance

_analysis.summary: This paper explores whether artificial systems can develop consciousness by proposing a new method called 'emergent language' (EL) in multi-agent reinforcement learning. Instead of relying on human-designed language or predefined concepts, EL lets AI agents develop communication from scratch under task-based pressure, avoiding biases from human priors. The goal is to determine if observed structures in AI systems are genuine or artifacts of human influence.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper focuses on AI consciousness rather than competition, its exploration of AI agents developing language independently could signal future shifts in AI capabilities that may impact digital market dynamics.
_analysis.market_signal: Future AI systems with emergent communication could reduce reliance on human-designed interfaces, potentially disrupting how digital platforms (e.g., search engines or social media) control user interactions.

items.title: LatentWave: JEPA Pretraining for Wireless Foundation Models
items.abstract: Wireless foundation models have emerged as a promising alternative to building separate models for each wireless task. However, existing approaches rely on masked input reconstruction, which can bias representations toward low-level signal details. In this paper, we propose LatentWave, a wireless foundation model pretrained using a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) on diverse wireless spectrograms and channel state information (CSI). By predicting masked regions in latent space, LatentWave learns representations that are more transferable out of the box across diverse downstream tasks. The proposed architecture employs per-channel patch embeddings with stochastic channel sampling during pretraining, allowing it to process variable antenna counts and improving usability across heterogeneous wireless configurations. We evaluate LatentWave on four downstream tasks: RF signal classification, 5G NR positioning, beam prediction, and LoS/NLoS classification, comparing against a masked-modeling baseline (WavesFM) pretrained on the same data. Additionally, we show that the masking geometry introduces a task-dependent inductive bias: frequency masking strongly favors channel-rel

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces LatentWave, a new wireless foundation model (a type of AI designed to work across multiple wireless communication tasks) that uses a technique called Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) to learn more generalizable representations from wireless data like spectrograms and channel state information (CSI). Unlike traditional models that focus on low-level signal details, LatentWave predicts masked regions in latent space, which could make it more adaptable for different wireless tasks without heavy retraining.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This work signals the growing importance of AI-driven wireless technologies, which could disrupt telecom markets and challenge existing infrastructure providers by enabling more flexible, software-defined solutions.
_analysis.market_signal: The development of foundation models for wireless communications may reduce reliance on proprietary hardware, potentially lowering barriers to entry for new competitors in telecom infrastructure.

items.title: An Infectious Disease Spread Simulation Based on Large Language Model Decision Making
items.abstract: Modelling individual decision-making during infectious disease outbreaks is crucial for understanding behavioural dynamics and informing effective public health interventions. Prior work has shown that large language models can simulate realistic human behaviour by generating agent decisions based on demographic prompts and situational context. We build on this foundation with a spatially grounded, agent-based simulation framework that integrates LLM-generated decisions about self-reported influenza-like illness into a census-based synthetic population of agents. Location is treated as a central feature: agents are assigned to spatial units within cities, capturing the spatial distributions of different demographic groups using real-world census data and enabling geographically diverse behavioural modelling. We implement and compare three decision scenarios, independent reasoning, household influence, and message framing, and simulate self-reporting outcomes in San Francisco and Atlanta. Results reveal that income and education are the dominant drivers of reporting rate variation, with smaller but consistent effects from geography, LLM model choice, and message framing. Our framewo

_analysis.summary: This academic paper explores how large language models (LLMs) can simulate human decision-making during infectious disease outbreaks by generating realistic agent behaviors based on demographic and situational context. The study uses a spatially grounded, agent-based simulation framework to model self-reported illness decisions within a synthetic population, demonstrating the potential of LLMs to replicate complex social dynamics.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper focuses on public health modeling, it highlights the growing capability of LLMs to simulate human-like decision-making, which could indirectly influence digital market dynamics by enabling more sophisticated AI-driven agents in other sectors.
_analysis.market_signal: The paper signals the increasing sophistication of AI agents, which may eventually disrupt digital markets by enabling more autonomous and human-like interactions in online platforms.

items.title: F3-Tokenizer: Taming Audio Autoencoder Latents for Understanding and Generation
items.abstract: Continuous audio autoencoders reconstruct waveforms well but often produce latents with weak structure for understanding, while self-supervised audio encoders capture semantics but are not directly decodable. This mismatch complicates a single audio tokenizer that must support both understanding and generation. We adapt continuous autoencoder latents to this setting with two components: a noise-regularized autoencoder bottleneck and a latent-side representation encoder. The bottleneck uses channel normalization and stochastic perturbation instead of KL-based variational training, yielding scale-controlled continuous latents for reconstruction and autoregressive generation. The representation encoder is trained on frozen autoencoder latents with RQ-MTP and frozen-LLM supervision. The resulting tokenizer provides high-dimensional representations for understanding while preserving normalized continuous latents as generation targets

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces F3-Tokenizer, a new method to improve audio autoencoders—AI systems that compress audio into a structured format for both understanding (e.g., speech recognition) and generation (e.g., text-to-speech). The authors propose a noise-regularized bottleneck and a latent-side encoder to make audio latents more interpretable while maintaining high-quality reconstruction, addressing a key challenge in AI audio processing.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper focuses on technical advancements in AI audio processing, it indirectly highlights the growing importance of multimodal AI systems (combining audio, text, and vision) which could influence competition in digital markets where such technologies are deployed.
_analysis.market_signal: Advances in multimodal AI tokenization may accelerate the integration of audio AI into consumer and enterprise products, potentially reshaping competition in voice interfaces and audio content platforms.

items.title: Attack Detection using Time Series Foundation Models
items.abstract: This paper addresses the problem of attack detection in cyber-physical systems without any knowledge of the plant model or its structure. A remotely located plant transmits sensor measurements to an operator over a network that is assumed to be under attack. We consider two classes of attacks: model-free replay attacks and model-based stealthy attacks. For the latter, we derive closed-form expressions for the optimal stealthy attack policy against a $χ^2$ detector, for both linear and nonlinear systems. We then propose a model-structure-free detector based on TimesFM, a time-series foundation model developed by Google Research, which serves as a surrogate residual generator operating in a zero-shot fashion. We show empirically that the TimesFM-based detector achieves a comparable or superior attack detection performance. The efficacy of the proposed approach is demonstrated numerically on the IEEE 14-bus power system. We also demonstrate that TimesFM predictions can serve as a substitute for corrupted measurements, a practical mitigation technique when classical redundancy assumptions fail.

_analysis.summary: This paper develops a method to detect cyberattacks on industrial systems by using a time-series foundation model called TimesFM, which does not require prior knowledge of the system's structure. It analyzes two types of attacks—replay attacks (where attackers replay old sensor data) and stealthy attacks (where attackers manipulate data to avoid detection)—and provides mathematical tools to identify optimal stealthy attack strategies against a common detection method ($χ^2$ detector).
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper focuses on cybersecurity for industrial systems, its use of foundation models like TimesFM signals broader trends in AI-driven detection systems that could impact digital market competition, particularly in critical infrastructure and cloud services.
_analysis.market_signal: The adoption of time-series foundation models for attack detection may accelerate the consolidation of AI-powered cybersecurity services, potentially reinforcing the dominance of large tech firms with access to such models.

items.title: Equivariant Neural Belief Propagation
items.abstract: Probabilistic inference over spatially embedded variables requires beliefs that respect $SE(3)$ symmetry, yet existing equivariant networks produce only scalars and vectors -- not the rank-2 precision tensors needed for anisotropic uncertainty, and single-component messages collapse multi-modal energy landscapes to physically meaningless averages. We introduce Equivariant Neural Belief Propagation (ENBP), a factor-graph framework whose messages are equivariant Gaussian mixture models with sufficient statistics that transform exactly under $SE(3)$. Rank-2 precision matrices are synthesised via equivariant outer products, ingested through differentiable spectral decomposition, and kept tractable by a greedy KL-based mixture reduction that provably commutes with $SE(3)$. On GEOM-QM9 and GEOM-Drugs, ENBP achieves 98.9% conformational coverage at 0.090 $\mathring{A}$ error with sub-second latency -- over $100\times$ faster than diffusion baselines at higher accuracy. On multi-body robotic inference, vanilla loopy BP diverges at 15+ agents while ENBP converges with near-zero collision rates and machine-precision equivariance error (${\sim}10^{-7}$ vs.\ $10^{-1}$ for augmented baselines).

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces Equivariant Neural Belief Propagation (ENBP), a new method for probabilistic inference that respects $SE(3)$ symmetry (rotational and translational invariance in 3D space). It addresses limitations in existing equivariant networks by generating rank-2 precision tensors (mathematical tools to represent anisotropic uncertainty) and avoiding collapsing multi-modal energy landscapes (complex probability distributions) into meaningless averages.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper advances AI techniques for spatial data, it does not directly address digital competition policy, market structure, or antitrust concerns.
_analysis.market_signal: This research signals potential improvements in AI-driven spatial modeling and simulation, which could indirectly impact industries like robotics or autonomous systems but has no immediate bearing on digital markets or competition.

items.title: Symmetric Divergence and Normalized Similarity: A Unified Topological Framework for Representation Analysis
items.abstract: Topological Data Analysis (TDA) offers a principled, intrinsic lens for comparing neural representations. However, existing paired topological divergences (e.g., RTD) are limited by heuristic asymmetry and, more critically, unbounded scores that depend on sample size, hindering reliable cross-scenario benchmarking. To address these challenges, we develop a unified topological toolkit serving two complementary needs: fine-grained structural diagnosis and robust, standardized evaluation. First, we complete the RTD framework by introducing Symmetric Representation Topology Divergence (SRTD) and its efficient variant SRTD-lite. Beyond resolving the theoretical asymmetry of prior variants, SRTD consolidates diagnostic information into a single, comprehensive cross-barcode signature. This allows for precise localization of structural discrepancies and serves as an effective optimization objective without the overhead of dual directional computations. Second, to enable reliable benchmarking across heterogeneous settings, we propose Normalized Topological Similarity (NTS). By measuring the rank correlation of hierarchical merge orders, NTS yields a scale-invariant metric bounded between -1

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces a new method called Symmetric Representation Topology Divergence (SRTD) to better compare how AI models process and represent data. It fixes flaws in older methods that gave unfair or inconsistent results, making it easier to evaluate AI systems fairly across different scenarios.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While not directly about competition policy, this paper's tools for evaluating AI model representations could indirectly affect how regulators assess AI-driven market power or dominance in digital markets.
_analysis.market_signal: Improved AI evaluation methods may accelerate the adoption of more transparent and comparable AI systems, potentially influencing how regulators scrutinize AI-powered platforms.

items.title: TokenMizer: Graph-Structured Session Memory for Long-Horizon LLM Context Management
items.abstract: Large language model (LLM) deployments for long-horizon tasks face a fundamental constraint: context windows are finite while productive work sessions are not. When history exceeds the Maximum Effective Context Window (MECW), critical structured information - architectural decisions, task transitions, file histories - is silently discarded. Existing mitigations treat history as flat text, destroying the relational structure that makes sessions resumable. We present TokenMizer, an open-source proxy system that models LLM session history as a typed knowledge graph. The schema defines 14 node types and 7 edge types. A hybrid extraction pipeline populates the graph incrementally, while a three-tier checkpoint system serializes it into compact resume blocks. An 8-layer compression pipeline reduces context overhead, and a semantic cache reduces repeated-query latency. Evaluated on a controlled benchmark of 21 sessions spanning 5 domains, TokenMizer demonstrates significant token economy. It produces resume blocks averaging 78 tokens (range: 42-124) - 2x smaller than evaluated baselines (159-170 tokens) - while achieving higher decision recall (+9-17 percentage points). Crucially, baselin

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces TokenMizer, a system that helps large language models (LLMs) manage long-term memory by organizing session history into a structured knowledge graph instead of flat text. This approach preserves relational context (e.g., task transitions, file histories) that would otherwise be lost when context windows are exceeded.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: Demonstrates how improved LLM memory management could reduce reliance on dominant cloud providers or proprietary platforms for session resumption, potentially lowering switching costs and increasing competition in AI services.
_analysis.market_signal: Advances in LLM context management may enable more independent, long-horizon AI agents, reducing dependence on gatekeeper platforms like cloud providers or search engines.

items.title: Bridging Domain Expertise and Generalization for Performance Estimation
items.abstract: Performance estimation under distribution shift aims to predict how a model behaves on an unlabeled test set whose distribution differs from the training data, a scenario that requires reliable indicators that can faithfully reflect model behavior without ground-truth labels. Existing approaches rely solely on the outputs of the given model whose biases are amplified once the distribution shifts, weakening the correlation with the true performance. Motivated by this limitation, we propose Fused Reference Alignment Prediction (FRAP), which leverages the complementary strengths of an external foundation model and the base model to construct a more reliable surrogate of the ground-truth labels. FRAP aligns the prediction distribution of the foundation model with that of the base model by applying temperature-scaled calibration that minimizes their divergence. The aligned predictions are fused through confidence-based weighting into a refined reference distribution that integrates robustness from the foundation model and domain-specific expertise from the base model, and performance estimation is obtained by measuring how closely the base model predictions agree with this reference. Ex

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces FRAP (Fused Reference Alignment Prediction), a new method to estimate how AI models perform when tested on data that differs from their training set. It addresses a key problem where AI models often give unreliable performance estimates under real-world conditions, which could mislead businesses or regulators about their true capabilities.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: FRAP's ability to improve performance estimation under distribution shifts could reveal hidden weaknesses in AI systems used by dominant platforms, impacting market power assessments.
_analysis.market_signal: Improved AI performance estimation methods may expose vulnerabilities in large platforms' AI systems, potentially enabling new competitors to challenge incumbents.

items.title: Subspace-Aware Sparse Autoencoders for Effective Mechanistic Interpretability
