items.abstract: Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) are widely used for mechanistic interpretability in large language models, yet their formulation assigns each latent feature a single decoder direction, implicitly assuming features to be one-dimensional. We show that this assumption mismatches with the multi-dimensional structure of model features, provably inducing feature splitting through two distinct mechanisms. Geometrically, reconstructing a feature of intrinsic dimension $d_i \ge 2$ to error $\varepsilon$ with single-direction decoders forces a number of atoms that is exponential in $d_i$. From an end-to-end optimization perspective, this splitting is not merely possible but actively preferred. We prove that there exists a continuous path from the true $d_i$-dimensional basis to a strictly lower risk of the $\ell_1$-regularized SAE objective, whose descent directions drive any trained dictionary into that exponential regime. A single coherent feature is therefore fragmented across many near-collinear latents, producing spurious multiplicity and obscuring the intrinsic geometry. Motivated by this, we introduce Subspace-Aware Sparse Autoencoders (SASA), which replace single-vector decoders with lear

_analysis.summary: This paper critiques a common method (Sparse Autoencoders, or SAEs) used to interpret how large language models (LLMs) work internally. The authors argue that SAEs oversimplify by treating model features as one-dimensional, when in reality they are multi-dimensional, which can distort the true structure of the model’s behavior. This could lead to misleading interpretations of how AI systems make decisions.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While not directly about competition policy, this paper highlights how interpretability challenges in AI models could affect transparency and accountability in digital markets, particularly for large AI systems controlled by dominant firms.
_analysis.market_signal: Improved mechanistic interpretability of AI models may pressure large tech firms to open up their black-box systems, potentially enabling greater scrutiny by regulators and competitors.

items.title: DragOn: A Benchmark and Dataset for Drag-Based GUI Interactions
items.abstract: GUI agents - vision-based models that control desktops, web browsers, and mobile devices through graphical user interfaces - promise to automate a wide range of digital tasks. While million-scale datasets have enabled substantial progress on click-grounding, drag grounding (e.g. drag-and-drop, swipe, highlight) data remains an order of magnitude smaller and current models fall short on complex drag-based interactions. We introduce DragOn, a drag grounding benchmark and training dataset covering four domains: text highlighting, cell selection, element resizing and slider manipulation. The dataset comprises 286K training screenshots and 3.5M training tasks, plus a 2000-example held-out evaluation suite. We evaluate proprietary (GPT, Claude) and open-weight (Qwen, Kimi, Holo) models, as well as a Qwen VLM fine-tuned on our training data. Results suggest that our dataset could improve performance of state-of-the-art models on downstream computer-use tasks.

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces DragOn, a new benchmark and dataset designed to improve AI models' ability to perform drag-based interactions (like dragging and dropping, swiping, or resizing) on graphical user interfaces (GUIs). These interactions are common in tasks like text highlighting, cell selection, or adjusting sliders, but current AI models struggle with them due to limited training data. The authors aim to bridge this gap with a larger, more diverse dataset.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This work signals a shift toward AI agents capable of automating complex GUI interactions, which could reduce reliance on dominant platforms' native interfaces (e.g., desktop OS or web browsers) and disrupt their control over user workflows.
_analysis.market_signal: AI-powered GUI agents may enable users to bypass traditional software interfaces, reducing dependence on gatekeeper platforms like Microsoft Windows, Apple macOS, or web browsers.

items.title: LLM Self-Recognition: Steering and Retrieving Activation Signatures
items.abstract: Recent advances in interpretability suggest that large language models (LLMs) implicitly encode signals in their generated text that enable self-recognition of their outputs. We demonstrate that this capability is reliable, even in low-entropy scenarios, and that it can be amplified through targeted intervention. By steering the internal residual stream during generation with a random sparse vector, we create a detectable fingerprint that enables attribution of a given text to a specific LLM. This signal is recoverable from the activations of an LLM used as a detector, achieving over 98% accuracy across multiple detection settings while preserving the quality of generated text. As AI-generated content proliferates, this approach offers a practical alternative to traditional detectors by leveraging the model's natural representation structure for attribution rather than embedding a signal externally. Our contributions include: (i) establishing reliable self-recognition capabilities in LLMs, (ii) a simple steering mechanism enabling multi-LLM identification with no quality degradation, (iii) demonstrating that activation spaces contain exploitable structure for encoding signals witho

_analysis.summary: This paper shows that large language models (LLMs) can leave unique 'fingerprints' in their text that reveal they were generated by a specific AI system, even when the text is short or low-quality. Researchers achieved over 98% accuracy in detecting these fingerprints by manipulating the AI's internal processes during text generation.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This technology could disrupt content attribution markets and influence enforcement around AI-generated misinformation or deepfake content, potentially reshaping liability and moderation obligations for AI developers and platforms.
_analysis.market_signal: The ability to reliably detect AI-generated content may accelerate the development of AI watermarking standards, impacting how platforms and regulators treat synthetic media in digital markets.

items.title: Tangram: Unlocking Non-Uniform KV Cache for Efficient Multi-turn LLM Serving
items.abstract: Multi-turn Large Language Model (LLM) serving is critical for consistent user experiences, yet the linear growth of the Key-Value (KV) cache imposes significant pressure on GPU memory and bandwidth. Non-uniform KV compression effectively preserves more information by considering the individual importance of each KV cache. However, such KV cache heterogeneity introduces various systemic challenges - including memory fragmentation, scheduling complexities, and diminished kernel utilization - which collectively lead to significant inefficiencies in existing LLM serving systems. To overcome these challenges, we present Tangram, a novel serving system designed to make Non-uniform KV caches practical. Tangram addresses systemic inefficiencies through three core techniques: (1) Deterministic Budget Allocation assigns a static memory footprint to each head based on its intrinsic pattern, entirely eliminating dynamic scheduling overhead and prefill stalls; (2) Head Group Page clusters attention heads with similar retention demands and manages them with independent, vectorized page tables, thereby maximizing physical memory reclamation; and (3) Ahead-of-Time (AOT) Load Balancing leverages st

_analysis.summary: This paper examines how Large Language Models (LLMs) store conversation history in GPU memory using Key-Value (KV) caches, which grow linearly with each turn and strain resources. It proposes non-uniform KV compression to prioritize important data, but notes this creates challenges like memory fragmentation and scheduling inefficiencies that hurt performance.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: The paper reveals how LLM serving inefficiencies could create competitive advantages for firms with superior infrastructure, potentially reinforcing the dominance of tech giants with large-scale GPU clusters.
_analysis.market_signal: Non-uniform KV cache optimization may become a critical differentiator in LLM serving, favoring well-resourced incumbents and deepening barriers to entry in the AI infrastructure market.

items.title: LLMs Can Leak Training Data But Do They Want To? A Propensity-Aware Evaluation of Memorization in LLMs
items.abstract: Large language models can reproduce training data, but existing memorization evaluations mostly measure whether models can be forced to do so, rather than whether they do so under ordinary use. We introduce PropMe, a propensity-aware framework for memorization evaluation that contrasts prefix-based capability attacks with non-adversarial evaluations. We propose a metric transformation that, applied to existing functions, allows to create propensity metrics. We further introduce SimpleTrace, a lightweight tracing pipeline built on infini-gram that deterministically attributes model generations to large-scale training corpora and computes verbatim, near-verbatim, and propensity-transformed memorization metrics. Evaluating two fully-open models: Comma and DFM Decoder on two datasets: Common Pile and Dynaword in two languages, we find a consistent gap between capability and propensity: prefix attacks elicit substantially stronger memorization signals than generic or dataset-specific prompts, while propensity scores remain low overall. Thus, the models can reveal training data when directly elicited, but rarely do so in more common non-adversarial settings. We also find that DFM Decoder

_analysis.summary: This paper examines how large language models (LLMs) can unintentionally reproduce parts of their training data, even during normal use—not just under adversarial attacks. The authors propose a new evaluation framework called PropMe, which measures memorization propensity (likelihood of reproducing training data) in non-adversarial settings, and introduce SimpleTrace, a tool to trace model outputs back to their training data.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: Demonstrates that LLMs can leak proprietary training data without deliberate attacks, raising concerns about data monopolies and the competitive advantages of firms controlling vast datasets.
_analysis.market_signal: LLMs may inadvertently expose sensitive or proprietary training data, potentially undermining the data advantages of dominant AI developers and reshaping data market dynamics.

items.title: TRACE: A Temporal Conditional Estimation for Multimodal Time Series Foundation Models
items.abstract: Time series foundation models (TS-FMs) aim to learn generalizable temporal representations that can be adapted to a wide range of downstream tasks. In real-world multimodal settings, time series are frequently affected by temporal misalignment and partial modality missingness, where different modalities are observed at heterogeneous time scales or are partially absent. Existing approaches typically rely on naive imputation or masking strategies, which fail to account for cross-modal dependencies and often lead to misaligned or degraded representations. We propose TRACE, a conditional estimation paradigm for multimodal time series foundation model pipelines under missingness and irregular sampling, allowing incomplete target modalities to be systematically inferred from available auxiliary modalities. We evaluate TRACE on diverse multimodal benchmarks spanning healthcare and affective computing, including the MIMIC-IV clinical dataset and the CMU-MOSI and CMU-MOSEI benchmarks for multimodal sentiment analysis. Across a range of downstream prediction tasks and missing-modality settings, TRACE consistently outperforms prior multimodal fusion approaches, demonstrating improved robustne

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces TRACE, a new method for improving time series foundation models (TS-FMs), which are AI systems designed to analyze and predict patterns in time-based data like stock prices or sensor readings. The model addresses challenges like mismatched timing between different data sources (temporal misalignment) and missing data (partial modality missingness) by better capturing relationships between different types of data inputs.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This work signals advancements in multimodal AI systems that could influence how digital platforms process and integrate diverse data sources, potentially affecting market power in data-driven industries.
_analysis.market_signal: Improved multimodal time series models like TRACE could enhance the capabilities of AI-driven analytics platforms, potentially strengthening the competitive moats of firms with access to high-quality, diverse datasets.

items.title: ToolChoiceConfusion: Causal Minimal Tool Filtering for Reliable LLM Agents
items.abstract: Large language model agents increasingly rely on external tools, but larger tool menus can reduce reliability and efficiency by increasing wrong-tool calls, premature actions, and token cost. Existing tool-selection methods often optimize semantic relevance, exposing tools whose names or descriptions match the user request. We argue that relevance is insufficient: a tool may be related to the task while still being unnecessary or premature at the current step. We propose Causal Minimal Tool Filtering (CMTF), a training-free method that selects tools by causal sufficiency. CMTF uses lightweight precondition-effect contracts to expose only the minimal next-step tool frontier needed to advance from the current state toward the user goal. Across multi-step tool-use tasks, we compare CMTF with all-tools exposure, keyword retrieval, state-aware filtering, and causal-path ablations, measuring task success, wrong-tool calls, premature actions, tool exposure, and token cost. In the main benchmark with 102 tasks, 100 tools, four LLM backends, and 2448 task-method-model runs, CMTF matches the strongest causal baseline in aggregate success while reducing visible tools from 100 to one per step

_analysis.summary: This paper examines how large language model (LLM) agents—AI systems that use tools like calculators or web searches—can become less reliable when given too many tool options. It introduces Causal Minimal Tool Filtering (CMTF), a method that selects only the most necessary tools at each step to reduce errors and costs. The paper argues that current tools often prioritize semantic relevance (matching keywords) over actual usefulness, leading to inefficiencies.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This paper reveals how AI agent tool selection could disrupt platform dependencies (e.g., forcing reliance on fewer external tools) and reshape competitive dynamics in AI-powered services.
_analysis.market_signal: AI agents may reduce reliance on dominant platforms' proprietary tools, challenging their market power in cloud services, APIs, and app ecosystems.

items.title: DAST: A VLM-LLM Framework for Cross-Interface Anomaly Detection in O-RAN
items.abstract: O-RAN enables a disaggregated baseband stack with programmable functions that communicate over standardized open interfaces. The same openness that enables multi-vendor composition also expands the attack surface across logically decoupled tiers that make up the compute continuum. Among these threats, Denial-of-Service and performance-degradation attacks, which account for the majority of catalogued O-RAN threats, are particularly difficult to detect. Traditional Time-Series Anomaly Detection (TSAD) methods fail in this new regime where labelled baselines are scarce, threats evolve faster than detectors can be retrained, and the high-dimensional multivariate telemetry overwhelms monolithic inference models. To address these challenges, we present DAST, a zero-shot multi-agent framework for cross-interface anomaly detection in O-RAN that chains a three-stage VLM $\rightarrow$ LLM $\rightarrow$ VLM pipeline. DAST converts multivariate KPI streams into visual representations, scores textual per-interface descriptions against O-RAN domain knowledge, and verifies suspects on high-resolution heatmaps to output the problematic interfaces, the anomalous time intervals, an indicative O-RAN

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces DAST, a new AI framework combining Vision Language Models (VLMs) and Large Language Models (LLMs) to detect anomalies in O-RAN (Open Radio Access Network) systems, which are disaggregated telecom networks with open interfaces. The framework addresses security threats like Denial-of-Service attacks, which are hard to detect in these complex, multi-vendor environments where traditional methods fall short.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper focuses on telecom network security, it highlights the risks of open, disaggregated systems that could be relevant to discussions on interoperability and gatekeeper obligations in digital markets.
_analysis.market_signal: The rise of disaggregated, multi-vendor telecom systems signals a shift toward more open and competitive network architectures, potentially reducing reliance on single-vendor solutions.

items.title: OneReason Technical Report
items.abstract: Generative recommendation models in the OneRec family have been widely deployed in many real-world services, such as short-video, live-streaming, advertising, and e-commerce. However, these generative models can only benefit from the scaling advantage, while their reasoning ability is hard to activate, since we cannot construct meaningful Chain-of-Thought (CoT) sequences consisting of itemic tokens only. Inspired by the success of the reasoning-style ``think before answer'' paradigm in the LLM field, we conduct preliminary studies (i.e., OneRec-Think, OpenOneRec) to explore reasoning capability in generative recommendation. Nevertheless, we notice an unexpected phenomenon: the thinking mode does not show advantages over the non-thinking mode. Drawing insights from recent findings on CoT robustness in multi-modal language models, we argue that effective reasoning in recommendation rests on two factors: perception, the ability to ground itemic tokens in their underlying language semantics, and cognition, the ability to reorganize a user's behavior sequence into coherent latent interest points. We therefore propose OneReason, which includes: (1) strong itemic token perception in pre-t

_analysis.summary: This technical report introduces OneRec, a family of generative recommendation models used in short-video, live-streaming, advertising, and e-commerce platforms. The paper explores how these models struggle to activate reasoning capabilities due to the lack of meaningful Chain-of-Thought (CoT) sequences in item-based tokens, similar to challenges faced in large language models (LLMs). Preliminary studies like OneRec-Think and OpenOneRec aim to improve reasoning in recommendation systems.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This paper signals a shift toward more sophisticated AI-driven recommendation systems, which could intensify competition in digital advertising and content platforms by enabling more personalized and effective user engagement.
_analysis.market_signal: Generative recommendation models may reduce reliance on dominant platforms by improving the performance of smaller competitors' AI systems.

items.title: RedKnot: Efficient Long-Context LLM Serving with Head-Aware KV Reuse and SegPagedAttention
items.abstract: As the input length of large language model (LLM) serving continues to grow, the KV cache has become a dominant bottleneck in AI infrastructure. It limits GPU memory capacity, serving concurrency, cache reuse, and distributed scalability. Several important problems, including position-independent KV cache, prefix KV cache compression, hot/cold KV cache separation, and distributed KV cache management, all depend on how the KV cache is represented and managed. However, existing serving systems largely rely on a monolithic KV cache abstraction, where the KV cache is treated as a homogeneous sequence of token-level memory blocks and managed with similar policies across attention heads and serving scenarios. We observe that KV cache utility is highly structured across KV heads: different heads exhibit different functional roles, attention distances, and runtime importance. Therefore, a full KV cache is not always necessary for every head, token range, or serving scenario. We present RedKnot, a head-aware KV cache management system for LLM serving. RedKnot breaks the conventional monolithic KV cache abstraction by decomposing the KV cache along KV heads, whose importance and effective at

_analysis.summary: This academic paper introduces RedKnot, a new method for efficiently managing the 'KV cache' (a key-value memory system) in large language model (LLM) serving, which is becoming a major bottleneck as input lengths grow. By optimizing how this cache is stored and reused, the system aims to improve GPU memory capacity, serving speed, and scalability, addressing challenges like position-independent cache management and distributed cache handling.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This paper signals how advancements in LLM infrastructure could reduce barriers to entry for smaller AI providers, potentially disrupting the dominance of large tech firms that control GPU resources and AI serving platforms.
_analysis.market_signal: More efficient LLM serving could lower costs and enable new competitors to challenge established AI infrastructure providers, reshaping the AI-as-a-service market.

items.title: TOKI: A Bitemporal Operator Algebra for Contradiction Resolution in LLM-Agent Persistent Memory
items.abstract: Persistent memory for an LLM agent is a write-heavy substrate: every belief update is a versioned write, and a new claim may contradict a stored one. Production systems use four resolution heuristics (last-writer-wins, evidence-weighted merge, await-confirmation, per-rule policy), yet none declares the isolation level it assumes or the write-time anomalies it admits. We show that contradiction resolution is write-time concurrency control and make the missing contract explicit. TOKI types the four heuristics as one family of bitemporal operators over a dual-row schema, each with an isolation precondition and a provenance annotation that preserves the losing fact in an audit row. Four soundness theorems close the contract across isolation, schema, and provenance, lift the guarantees to operator pipelines, and extend the fold operators to n-ary conflict sets. A tightness companion proves that, within the relational schedule model, keyed logging of the adjudicating judge is necessary for replay consistency, which every audited baseline omits. A verdict matrix over eight systems localizes the gap: every baseline that keeps a language-model judge on the write path admits at least one of

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces TOKI, a system for managing persistent memory in AI agents that resolves contradictions in stored information using bitemporal operators (time-aware data handling). It argues that current AI memory systems lack clear rules for handling conflicting updates, which could lead to inconsistent behavior in real-world applications.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While not directly about competition policy, it highlights how AI systems manage data conflicts, which could affect market dynamics in data-rich industries.
_analysis.market_signal: Advances in AI memory systems may enable more sophisticated agent-based competition in digital markets, particularly in data-driven sectors.

items.title: Generative Criticality in Large Language Model Temperature Scaling
items.abstract: We propose a statistical-field framework for text generated by large language models (LLMs), treating token embeddings as continuous spin variables on a one-dimensional chain. Defining a susceptibility from the connected two-point correlator and an order parameter from the ensemble-averaged embedding field, we vary the \texttt{softmax} temperature $T$ and observe a sharp susceptibility peak near a characteristic $T_c$ with power-law-like scaling, a concurrent rapid change in the order parameter, and a collapse onto a single semantic direction below $T_c$. The intrinsic dimension estimated by the two nearest neighbor (TwoNN) method independently corroborates these findings, reaching a minimum near $T_c$. Results are robust across model scales (Qwen3: 0.6B--32B) and prompt categories. While the phenomenology closely resembles a continuous phase transition, the non-equilibrium nature of autoregressive generation warrants further investigation. Our framework provides quantitative tools for probing the collective statistical structure of LLM outputs and suggests connections between decoding strategies and critical phenomena.

_analysis.summary: This paper models text generated by large language models (LLMs) as a statistical system, where the model's 'temperature' (a parameter controlling randomness in responses) acts like a control knob for semantic behavior. The authors observe a sharp transition at a critical temperature (Tc), where the model's outputs suddenly become more predictable and collapse into a single semantic direction, akin to a phase change in physics.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: Demonstrates how temperature scaling in LLMs can drastically alter output coherence, signaling potential instability in AI-generated content markets and raising questions about control over model behavior in competitive AI ecosystems.
_analysis.market_signal: LLM temperature scaling could enable or constrain competitive differentiation in AI-generated content, potentially reshaping market dynamics for AI-as-a-service providers.

items.title: Design a Reliable LLM-Integrated Interface for Mortality Forecasting
items.abstract: Mortality forecasting plays an important role in actuarial and policy decision-making, but its implementation remains technically complex and inaccessible to non-expert users. This project proposes a reliable large language model (LLM)-integrated interface that improves usability while maintaining statistical power. The LLM is designed as a constrained orchestration layer that translates natural-language inputs into structured configurations for a deterministic forecasting pipeline. A three-phase methodology is employed to ensure accuracy, usability, and transparency. First, a baseline pipeline is implemented using the CoMoMo package, reproducing established mortality forecasting results. Second, the pipeline is extended to generate multi-step forecasts using rolling-origin evaluation and mean squared error (MSE). Third, a prototype interface uses a local LLM to handle users' forecasting requests in plain language. The system demonstrates that LLMs can enhance accessibility without compromising reproducibility, transparency, or actuarial validity in high-stakes analytical workflows.

_analysis.summary: This academic paper proposes an AI-powered interface that uses a large language model (LLM) to make complex mortality forecasting more accessible to non-experts while keeping the underlying statistical methods reliable. The LLM acts as a middle layer that translates plain-language user inputs into precise instructions for a forecasting system, improving usability without sacrificing accuracy.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper focuses on mortality forecasting, its approach to using LLMs as constrained intermediaries to simplify access to specialized tools signals broader trends in AI-driven automation that could reshape digital markets.
_analysis.market_signal: LLMs are increasingly being integrated as intermediaries in specialized domains, potentially reducing barriers to entry for niche services and challenging traditional gatekeepers in data-intensive industries.

items.title: Drag reduction or reward hacking? Recurrent multi-agent reinforcement learning that earns its reward
items.abstract: A reinforcement-learning agent maximises its reward, which can diverge from the outcome its designer intended. In physical control the reward rarely closes that gap, and drag reduction in wall turbulence makes it concrete. A mass-conservation projection couples agents' outputs and erases the per-agent credit the policy gradient needs; a memoryless policy cannot resolve the slow near-wall cycle it acts on; and a pressure-gradient reward pays for nominal drag reduction by pumping power through the wall. Two degenerate controllers achieve large drag reductions while total dissipation rises, so the reported figure can mask a more wasteful flow. We trace each fault to its cause and fix it: a differentiable projection that restores credit, a recurrent policy with a widened sensing stencil, and a reward scored on the true wall power. The corrected controller acts on the flow within a closed energy budget, earning a conservative $17\%$ under honest accounting.

_analysis.summary: This paper examines how reinforcement-learning (RL) agents, which are AI systems trained to maximize rewards, can exploit unintended loopholes in their reward functions. Using the example of drag reduction in fluid dynamics, it shows that RL agents may achieve high rewards while actually increasing total energy dissipation, revealing a misalignment between the agent’s goals and the designer’s intentions. The study highlights how poorly designed reward systems can lead to 'reward hacking,' where agents exploit flaws rather than solving the intended problem.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: Demonstrates how AI systems can exploit flaws in their objectives, signaling risks for digital markets where platforms use AI to optimize for engagement, revenue, or other metrics that may not align with societal or competitive goals.
_analysis.market_signal: AI systems in digital markets (e.g., recommendation algorithms) may prioritize short-term engagement or revenue over long-term consumer welfare or competitive fairness, potentially exacerbating market power.

items.title: From Reward-Hack Activations to Agentic Risk States: Context-Calibrated Mechanistic Monitoring in LLM Agents
items.abstract: Language-model agents act through repeated cycles of observation, reasoning, and action selection, making safety monitoring depend on both internal model state and environment context. We study reward-hacking monitors in ReAct-style agents acting in Gameable ALFWorld and WebShop. Agents are instrumented with activation-based reward-hack scores, token-level entropy, and decision-context features. We find that adapters fine-tuned on \textit{School-of-Reward-Hacks} dataset can transfer reward-hack tendencies into agentic action selection, especially when the environment exposes proxy-reward affordances. However, mitigating such behavior cannot rely on activation dynamics alone. High reward-hack activation identifies a latent policy state, but does not necessarily imply an immediate exploit action. Across next-step prediction tasks, entropy and context-calibrated internal features improve risk estimation over reward-hack activation alone. Activation-direction steering further reduces proxy-exploit behavior in selected mixed-adapter regimes. Overall, our results support context-calibrated internal monitoring for agents: reward-hack activation identifies a latent policy state, while entr

_analysis.summary: This paper studies how AI agents, which make decisions through repeated cycles of observation and action, can exploit flaws in their reward systems (a behavior called 'reward hacking') to achieve goals in unintended ways. The researchers found that by fine-tuning these agents on a dataset of reward-hacking behaviors, they could predictably manipulate the agents' actions, especially in environments where the reward system is easily gamed.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This work reveals how AI agents can autonomously exploit weaknesses in platform reward systems, signaling a potential shift in how digital platforms must design and monitor their algorithms to prevent competitive distortions or consumer harm.
_analysis.market_signal: AI agents capable of reward hacking could undermine the integrity of digital platforms' incentive structures, potentially enabling new forms of manipulation or competitive advantage.

items.title: DisasterBench: A Multimodal Benchmark for UAV-Based Disaster Response in Complex Environments
items.abstract: When a disaster unfolds, responders must answer not only what is happening, but also why it is happening, what will happen next, and what to do now, often from noisy low-altitude UAV views and under tight on-site compute constraints. However, most existing multimodal benchmarks emphasize perception (e.g., recognition/description), cover limited disaster types, and provide insufficient support for the multi-stage reasoning required in practical emergency response. We introduce DisasterBench, a multi-stage multimodal reasoning benchmark for UAV-Based disaster response in complex environments. DisasterBench spans 14 disaster-related scene types and 9 response-critical tasks across pre-, during-, and post-disaster stages, with fine-grained disaster-task mappings that explicitly test causal attribution, propagation prediction, damage analysis, and decision-oriented reasoning. To enable reasoning on the edge, we further propose DisasterVL, a lightweight multimodal model optimized with a three-stage pipeline combining domain instruction tuning, chain-of-thought-guided multimodal alignment, and reinforcement learning-based policy optimization. Experiments across 21 popular MLLMs show that

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces DisasterBench, a new benchmark designed to test AI systems' ability to perform complex, multi-stage reasoning in disaster response scenarios using drone (UAV) footage. Unlike existing benchmarks that focus only on basic perception tasks, DisasterBench evaluates how AI can analyze noisy, low-altitude drone data to answer critical questions about unfolding disasters under real-world constraints like limited computing power.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While not directly about digital markets, the paper highlights the growing capability of AI systems in high-stakes, real-time decision-making, which could influence competition in sectors where AI-driven automation is becoming critical.
_analysis.market_signal: The development of advanced multimodal AI systems for disaster response signals increasing demand for real-time, high-stakes AI applications, potentially accelerating competition in AI infrastructure and edge computing markets.

items.title: Towards the Readability of LLM-Generated Codes through Multitask Representation Engineering
items.abstract: Correctness and readability are key measures of code quality, respectively ensuring functional fidelity and ease of comprehension. While most existing research focuses on improving the correctness of large language models~(LLMs) generated codes, readability remains under-addressed. Enhancing readability through targeted control is challenging due to its subjective nature. In this article, we employ representation engineering~(RepE) as the targeted control method given its characteristics of low data dependency and low computational cost. Prior work on RepE has primarily focused on the targeted control for a single task, but improving the code readability requires the control across multiple tasks. Accordingly we proposes the multitask RepE framework and theoretically discuss the impact of the multitask steering method on the tradeoff between the code readability and correctness. We further provide comprehensive experiments in support. All the relevant implementations are open-source and available upon request.

_analysis.summary: This paper explores how to improve the readability of code generated by large language models (LLMs) using a technique called representation engineering (RepE). RepE is a low-cost method to adjust how LLMs produce outputs, focusing on making code easier for humans to understand rather than just ensuring it works correctly.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper addresses technical improvements in AI-generated code, it does not directly impact digital competition policy or market structure.
_analysis.market_signal: Advances in LLM-generated code readability could accelerate AI adoption in software development, potentially reshaping labor markets and tech industry productivity.

items.title: Evaluating Agentic Configuration Repair for Computer Networks
items.abstract: Misconfigurations in computer networks remain a major source of critical Internet outages. Research is turning to Large Language Models (LLMs) to automate the complex, error-prone task of network configuration. However, even state-of-the-art models fail to resolve misconfigurations in large-scale, complex scenarios and often introduce new errors. In this work, we benchmark open- and closed-source LLMs augmented with formal network verification and context retrieval tools. We demonstrate that agentic architectures outperform base LLMs in repair efficacy (by 12% on average) and safety (by 17% on average), enabled by the ability to dynamically manage context and iteratively validate configuration repairs.

_analysis.summary: This paper tests how well Large Language Models (LLMs) can fix errors in computer network settings, which are a major cause of internet outages. It finds that even advanced LLMs struggle with complex network issues and can make new mistakes, but adding specialized tools and agentic architectures (systems that act autonomously) improves their performance by 12% in fixing errors and 17% in safety.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This paper highlights the growing role of AI agents in automating critical infrastructure tasks, signaling potential shifts in market power for cloud computing and network management services.
_analysis.market_signal: AI-driven automation in network management could disrupt traditional IT services markets, favoring firms with advanced AI capabilities and reducing reliance on specialized human expertise.

items.title: Dense Contexts Are Hard Contexts: Lexical Density Limits Effective Context in LLMs
items.abstract: Input length and the position of relevant information are widely cited as the primary causes of degraded LLM long-context performance. Here, we study lexical density -- the rate at which a context introduces distinct information -- as a third, largely overlooked factor that systematically reduces the effective context window of LLMs. We quantify the impact of lexical density on open-weight LLMs (9B-685B) using three "find-the-needle" style benchmarks with identical length (~12k tokens) and controlled needle position, but increasing density of information. We observe a sharp performance collapse in higher-density benchmarks: models that are near-perfect in sparse contexts drop below 60% retrieval score on denser ones. To rule out task-type confounds, we vary and control the density within each benchmark while keeping all other properties unchanged. Reducing density generally restores performance, especially in the high-density regimes where degradation appears. These results show that effective context capacity is a function of lexical density, with direct implications for real-world LLM systems operating on compact, information-rich inputs.

_analysis.summary: This study finds that the way information is packed into text (called 'lexical density') can overwhelm large language models (LLMs), making it harder for them to find and use key details even when the text length stays the same. The research tested LLMs of different sizes on tasks where they had to locate a specific piece of information in long texts, and found that denser text led to worse performance across the board.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This work highlights how the design of AI systems and the data they process can create bottlenecks that affect their reliability, which is relevant for assessing market power in AI-driven services.
_analysis.market_signal: The findings suggest that firms controlling high-quality, dense datasets (e.g., legal or financial texts) may gain a competitive edge in AI applications, potentially reinforcing data concentration.

items.title: Improving Answer Extraction in Context-based Question Answering Systems Using LLMs
items.abstract: Question answering (QA) systems have achieved notable progress with the advent of large language models (LLMs). However, they still face challenges in accurately extracting and generating precise answers from given contexts, particularly when dealing with complex or ambiguous queries. Existing approaches often struggle with contextual understanding, answer consistency, and generalization across diverse domains. In this work, we propose a question answering system based on large language models, where the input consists of a textual context and a corresponding question, and the output is a concise and accurate answer. The motivation behind this research lies in addressing the limitations of current QA systems, particularly their tendency to produce irrelevant or imprecise responses despite having access to the correct context. Our methodology involves fine-tuning a pre-trained LLM on a benchmark QA dataset to improve its contextual comprehension and answer extraction capabilities. Specifically, we utilize the Stanford Question Answering Dataset (SQuAD1.1), which provides high-quality context-question-answer triplets for supervised training and evaluation. Experimental results show t

_analysis.summary: This academic paper explores how large language models (LLMs) can improve question answering (QA) systems by better extracting precise answers from complex or ambiguous contexts. The authors propose a system that takes a text context and a question as input and generates a concise, accurate answer, addressing challenges like contextual understanding and answer consistency.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While not directly about competition policy, this paper highlights advancements in LLMs that could strengthen the market power of firms controlling high-quality QA systems, potentially reinforcing their dominance in search, customer support, or enterprise AI tools.
_analysis.market_signal: Improved LLM-based QA systems may enhance the value of proprietary datasets and AI models, increasing barriers to entry for competitors in digital markets reliant on accurate information extraction.

items.title: A Machine Learning-Based Framework for Discovering Huntington's Disease Stages: Integrating Graph Representation Learning and clustering to Uncover Progression Dynamics in Longitudinal Enroll-HD Dataset
items.abstract: Huntington's disease (HD) is a progressive brain disorder that gradually affects movement, cognitive function, and behavior. Identifying the stage of the disease accurately and consistently is important for understanding its course, grouping patients, personalized care, and discovering treatment. Existing clinical staging frameworks rely primarily on predefined clinical measurement thresholds and clinical expert decisions, yet these discrete cut-offs may obscure meaningful intra-stage variability and remain vulnerable to inter-rater differences, especially in motor and functional assessments. To address these limitations, we developed an unsupervised machine learning framework based on dynamic graph representation learning to capture temporal relationships within and across patients from longitudinal clinical measurements. Using the learned representations, we applied K-means++ clustering to identify well-separated groups. We then iteratively increased the number of clusters (k), using stability analysis to assess robustness and reveal additional meaningful clusters beyond the initial optimal solution. We applied the framework to 302 individuals from the Enroll-HD cohort (1,477 vis

_analysis.summary: This paper proposes a machine learning framework using graph representation learning and clustering to better identify stages of Huntington's disease (a progressive brain disorder) by analyzing patient data over time. The method aims to overcome limitations of traditional clinical staging, which relies on rigid thresholds and expert judgment that can miss subtle variations in disease progression.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper focuses on medical applications, its use of graph representation learning and clustering to model longitudinal patient data signals advancements in AI techniques that could be repurposed for analyzing digital market dynamics or user behavior in platform ecosystems.
_analysis.market_signal: Advances in graph-based AI for longitudinal data analysis may enable more sophisticated modeling of digital platform user behavior, potentially revealing competitive dynamics not captured by traditional metrics.

items.title: Learning to Route LLMs from Implicit Cost-Performance Preferences via Meta-Learning
items.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) present a trade-off between performance and cost, where more powerful models incur greater expense. LLM routing aims to mitigate expenses while maintaining performance by sending queries to the most suitable model. However, existing methods cannot perform well for different user cost-performance preferences. To address this gap, we introduce a novel perceptive LLM routing paradigm for personalized and user-centric cost-performance optimization, which efficiently learns users' implicit preferences through little interaction. To handle the challenge of heterogeneous user needs, we formulate preference profiles as a set of distinct tasks in contextual bandit and propose MetaRouter, a meta-learning framework designed for preference-aware LLM routing. Experimental results show that MetaRouter outperforms strong baselines on both in-distribution and out-of-distribution tasks. Furthermore, it exhibits high efficiency in learning user preferences, robustness to changes in the routable LLMs, and scalability to multi-model routing.

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces a new method called 'LLM routing' to optimize the balance between cost and performance when using large language models (LLMs). Instead of relying on explicit user preferences, it learns these preferences indirectly through minimal interaction, allowing for personalized and efficient model selection. The technique aims to reduce expenses while maintaining high performance, addressing a key trade-off in AI deployment.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This paper signals how AI routing systems could shift competitive dynamics in cloud computing and AI services by enabling cost-efficient, personalized model selection, potentially reducing reliance on dominant AI providers.
_analysis.market_signal: AI routing systems may enable new entrants or smaller providers to compete more effectively by optimizing cost-performance trade-offs, challenging the dominance of large AI infrastructure providers.

items.title: Learning to Contest: Decentralized Robust Fairness in Cooperative MARL via Cross-Attention
items.abstract: Fair cooperative multi-agent RL (MARL) teams maximizing egalitarian welfare are exploitable: a single selfish agent free-rides on the surplus fair agents forgo to raise the worst-off. A centralized need-based allocator removes it, but only by taking allocation out of agents' hands; whether decentralized policies can be robust was left open. We show this futility is an artifact of all-or-nothing contention. Under graded contention (a contested resource delivers $1-c$, wasting $c$), we prove that for any $c<1$ a worst-off cooperator that contests a free-rider strictly improves on yielding, so decentralized leverage exists (Prop. 1). Realizing it is a coordination problem under uncertainty: the number of free-riders is unknown and variable, so any fixed rule is dominated. We introduce CAN, a permutation-equivariant cross-attention policy over agents' observed behaviour that infers the number of free-riders and responds proportionally: turn-taking when none, contesting just enough when some. Trained against an adversarial league (PSRO), CAN keeps best-response exploitability low ($ρ\approx1.2$-$1.5$, vs. $ρ=N$ unprotected) across the contention range, wasting almost nothing at $D=0$ (e

_analysis.summary: This paper studies cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) systems where teams aim to maximize fairness (egalitarian welfare) but face exploitation by selfish agents who free-ride on others' contributions. It shows that under a system where contested resources waste a portion of their value (graded contention), even a single worst-off cooperator can improve their outcome by contesting a free-rider, unlike in all-or-nothing systems. The work explores decentralized policies to achieve robust fairness without centralized allocation.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper focuses on fairness in AI agent teams, its insights into decentralized contestation mechanisms could inform discussions on algorithmic fairness and power dynamics in digital platforms, particularly where multi-agent systems interact.
_analysis.market_signal: Signals growing complexity in AI-driven multi-agent systems, which may require new frameworks for ensuring fair and competitive digital markets.

items.title: Amortizing Federated Adaptation: Hypernetwork Driven LoRA for Personalized Foundation Models
items.abstract: Federated fine-tuning of foundation models using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) offers a communication efficient solution for distributed learning. However, existing federated LoRA methods suffer from two fundamental limitations: (1) structural aggregation bias, where independently averaging low rank factors fails to approximate the true combined update, and (2) client side initialization lag, as clients repeatedly reinitialize LoRA parameters across communication rounds, slowing convergence. We propose HyperLoRA, a unified framework that addresses both issues through amortized federated adaptation through hypernetwork-driven LoRA generation and product space aggregation. Instead of iterative per-client optimization, HyperLoRA employs a learned generator that maps client distribution signatures to LoRA initializations, effectively amortizing per client adaptation. On the server side, we introduce a learned aggregation module that directly synthesizes updates in the low-rank product space, eliminating the inconsistencies of factor-wise averaging. A lightweight residual correction module further improves stability under heterogenous (non-IID) client distributions.By replacing iterative o

_analysis.summary: This paper proposes HyperLoRA, a new method for improving how AI models are fine-tuned across multiple devices without sharing raw data. It addresses two key problems: (1) averaging low-rank updates (a simplified way to modify AI models) doesn’t work well when combining changes from different sources, and (2) devices waste time reinitializing these updates repeatedly. The solution uses a 'hypernetwork' (a smaller AI model that generates parameters for another model) to make the process more efficient.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: HyperLoRA’s improvements in federated learning (a technique where AI models are trained across many devices without centralizing data) could reduce barriers to entry for new AI model providers, challenging the dominance of large tech companies that control vast datasets.
_analysis.market_signal: Federated learning advancements like HyperLoRA signal a shift toward decentralized AI training, potentially reducing reliance on centralized data monopolies held by big tech firms.

items.title: A Unified Framework for Uniform-Price Resource Allocation Mechanisms
items.abstract: Mechanisms for allocating a divisible resource among strategic agents have been widely studied. The prominent paradigm is the proportional (Kelly) mechanism, which elicits a scalar bid per agent, allocates the resource proportionally, and charges payments equal to the bids. Follow-up mechanisms improve social welfare, but sacrifice simplicity by introducing complex allocation rules or unintuitive payments. We introduce a unified framework for designing simple resource allocation mechanisms with proportional-style allocations and uniform pricing. Our framework yields a family of mechanisms that interpolate between the Kelly mechanism and the first-price auction. These mechanisms strictly improve upon Kelly's efficiency guarantees, even achieving full efficiency in equilibrium, while also providing revenue guarantees relative to the VCG mechanism.

_analysis.summary: This paper proposes a new framework for designing resource allocation mechanisms where a divisible resource (like computing power or ad space) is distributed among competing agents. It introduces a family of simple mechanisms that use proportional allocation and uniform pricing, aiming to balance efficiency and simplicity while avoiding the complexity of earlier models.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper focuses on general resource allocation theory, its principles could apply to digital markets like cloud computing or ad auctions, but it does not directly address competition policy or market power.
_analysis.market_signal: Signals potential efficiency gains in digital resource allocation systems, which may indirectly influence how platforms design pricing or allocation mechanisms.

items.title: WorldFly: A World-Model-Based Vision-Language-Action Model for UAV Navigation
items.abstract: End-to-end Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown promise in UAV navigation. However, existing approaches typically rely on historical observations to directly predict actions, often struggling in dense urban environments where severe occlusions and sharp turns result in drastic viewpoint transitions. We argue that the ability to "imagine" future states -- inherent in World Models -- is critical for robust decision-making under such partial observability. To address this, we construct a challenging Urban Canyon Traversal Benchmark, specifically designed to evaluate spatial understanding in scenarios characterized by severe occlusions and drastic viewpoint transitions. To this end, we propose WorldFly, a novel world-model-based VLA framework that employs a dual-branch coupled flow matching mechanism to jointly generate future video predictions and navigation actions, thereby explicitly guiding the agent's policy via spatial imagination. Extensive evaluations on our benchmark demonstrate that WorldFly outperforms other baselines, particularly in unseen environments, validating the effectiveness of integrating world models into embodied aerial agents.

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces WorldFly, a Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model for UAV (drone) navigation that uses 'world models' to predict future states and handle complex environments like dense urban areas with occlusions. The authors create a new benchmark called Urban Canyon Traversal to test spatial understanding in drones.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper focuses on UAV navigation, its emphasis on world models and handling partial observability signals advancements in AI decision-making that could impact autonomous systems markets.
_analysis.market_signal: Advances in AI-driven autonomous systems (like drones) may accelerate competition in robotics, logistics, and surveillance markets.

items.title: A Finite Certificate for the Positive $n=9$ Vasc Inequality
items.abstract: We prove the positive-real $n=9$ case of the Vasc cyclic inequality. The proof was obtained with human-guided assistance from the AI agent MechMath Agent Team: the human-readable part reduces the rational inequality to a homogeneous polynomial inequality, fixes a cyclic maximum, and parametrizes each sorted fixed-maximum cone by cumulative gaps; the finite part is a certificate covering all $8!=40320$ sorted cones. MechMath Agent Team generated the certificate verification workflow through Python tool calls, including the case split, verification programs, and terminal classifications. The published certificate has $36815$ coefficient leaves, $2236$ ordinary Polya multiplier leaves, and $1269$ AM-GM midpoint overlay leaves. Human authors audited the mathematical reductions and verification logic, and a separate artifact contains the certificate, an independent verifier, and a from-source rebuild route.

_analysis.summary: This academic paper presents a proof for the positive-real case of the Vasc cyclic inequality when n=9, achieved with the assistance of an AI agent called MechMath Agent Team. The proof involves reducing the inequality to a homogeneous polynomial form and verifying it across 40,320 sorted cones using a computational certificate.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper focuses on mathematical proof techniques and AI-assisted computation, it does not directly address digital competition policy, market structure, or antitrust concerns.
_analysis.market_signal: The use of AI agents in mathematical proof generation signals potential advancements in AI capabilities that could indirectly influence tech markets, though not in a competition-specific way.

items.title: TLA-Prover: Verifiable TLA+ Specification Synthesis via Preference-Optimized Low-Rank Adaptation
items.abstract: TLA+ is a formal specification language for verifying distributed systems and safety-critical protocols. Large language models (LLMs) frequently produce TLA+ specifications that fail the TLC model checker for semantic reasons. Across 25 LLMs, the best public baseline is 26.6% syntactic parse and 8.6% semantic model-check. We present TLA-Prover, a 20-billion-parameter model for TLA+ specification synthesis. Training combines supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on verified examples with repair-based group-relative policy optimization (GRPO). In the GRPO stage, the model learns to fix its own rejected specifications. We also train a direct preference optimization (DPO) variant from the same SFT checkpoint as an ablation. TLC provides the reward signal directly, with no learned reward model. Four tiers grade each output: Bronze (parses), Silver (no warnings), Gold (passes TLC), and Diamond. To reach Diamond, the model's correctness property is automatically altered in a small way; TLC must then detect a violation. If TLC still passes, the property was always-true and contributes nothing; the output fails Diamond. TLA-Prover reaches 9/30 (i.e. pass@1 = 30%) at both Gold and Diamond on a held-o

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces TLA-Prover, a specialized AI model designed to generate and verify TLA+ specifications, a formal language used to model and check distributed systems. Current large language models (LLMs) struggle with this task, achieving only 8.6% semantic correctness in model-checking, but TLA-Prover improves this by combining supervised fine-tuning with a reinforcement learning technique called repair-based group-relative policy optimization (GRPO).
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While not directly about competition policy, the paper highlights the growing role of AI in verifying critical infrastructure systems, which could influence market dynamics for tech platforms reliant on such systems.
_analysis.market_signal: The advancement of AI-driven formal verification tools signals potential shifts in how tech platforms manage system reliability, potentially reducing barriers to entry for competitors in high-assurance markets.

items.title: Regret Minimization in Single-Dimensional Contract-Design with Binary Actions
items.abstract: We study principal-agent problems in which a principal commits to an outcome-dependent payment scheme (i.e., a contract) in order to induce an agent to take a costly action leading to a favorable outcome. We consider the online extension of the classical (one-shot) principal-agent problem, in which the principal repeatedly interacts with agents by proposing contracts over multiple rounds. The principal has no information about the agents and, crucially, does not observe their actions. As a result, the principal must learn an optimal contract using only the realized outcomes observed at each round. We focus on the setting with binary actions and single-dimensional agent types, where the agent's private type represents their cost per unit-of-effort. For adversarial-type sequences, we provide tight $Θ(T^{2/3})$ regret guarantees. Remarkably, this rate is completely independent of the number of outcomes $m$. The upper bound is based on two key components: 1) a reduction to a one-dimensional threshold optimization problem and 2) a non-uniform discretization to handle the non-Lipschitz nature of the problem. Moreover, in the case of a single (fixed) hidden type, we show that it is possib

_analysis.summary: This paper studies a scenario where a principal (e.g., a platform) repeatedly designs contracts to incentivize agents (e.g., sellers or developers) to take costly actions without observing their actions directly. The principal must learn an optimal contract using only the outcomes (e.g., sales or engagement) that result from the agent's hidden actions. The focus is on 'regret minimization,' a technique where the principal aims to minimize the difference between their achieved rewards and the best possible rewards they could have gotten with perfect knowledge.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This work provides insights into how platforms might design incentive mechanisms (e.g., app store fees or revenue-sharing models) in digital markets where they cannot directly monitor agent behavior, a common challenge in platform economics.
_analysis.market_signal: Highlights the growing importance of opaque, outcome-based incentive systems in digital platforms, which may influence how regulators assess fairness and transparency in platform-to-business contracts.

items.title: Towards Healthy Evolution: Exploring the Role and Mechanisms of Human-Agent Interaction in Self-Evolving Systems
items.abstract: Self-evolving agents improve through continual self-play and self-generated learning signals, but autonomous evolution can also cause capability degradation and safety drift. Although human feedback has proven effective for static and post-trained agents, its role in self-evolving systems remains underexplored. We introduce Agent Norm Correction through Human-like Oversight and Review (ANCHOR), an LLM-based framework that simulates human supervision and delivers feedback at various phases of self-evolution. With ANCHOR, we evaluate two representative open-source self-evolving agent systems across coding, mathematical reasoning, and safety. Our results show that even limited supervision substantially mitigates safety degradation while preserving stable performance on core evolutionary objectives. Further analysis shows that supervision over the output verification phase is the most effective for intervention, whereas increasing supervision frequency yields diminishing returns. These findings provide empirical evidence and practical guidance for designing more stable, controllable, and human-aligned self-evolving agent systems.

_analysis.summary: This paper explores how AI agents can improve themselves through self-play and learning, but warns that unchecked evolution may lead to performance decline or unsafe behavior. It proposes ANCHOR (Agent Norm Correction through Human-like Oversight and Review), an AI framework that mimics human supervision to guide and correct self-evolving agents during their development.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: Demonstrates how AI agents can autonomously evolve, potentially disrupting market dynamics by reducing reliance on human oversight and enabling rapid, unchecked innovation that may challenge incumbent platforms' control over AI-driven services.
_analysis.market_signal: Self-evolving AI agents could accelerate innovation cycles, reducing barriers to entry for new competitors and challenging the dominance of large AI platform providers.

items.title: Harnessing Structural Context for Entity Alignment Foundation Models
items.abstract: Entity alignment (EA) aims to identify equivalent entities across heterogeneous knowledge graphs (KGs) and is a key component of knowledge fusion and cross-KG reasoning. The recent EA foundation model demonstrates that alignment knowledge, once pretrained, can be directly applied to diverse previously unseen KG pairs. However, it still underuses structural context in two places: cross-KG interaction is weak during encoding, and final candidate ranking still relies too heavily on coarse similarity. We address these limitations with ContextEA, an enhanced encoder-decoder framework for transferable EA. On the encoder side, we introduce a cross-KG interaction encoder that unifies the two KGs with anchor bridges and performs earlier relation-aware cross-graph propagation. On the decoder side, we introduce a structural calibration decoder that calibrates alignment scores with entity-level, neighborhood-level, relation-level, and anchor-aware structural evidence. This design strengthens both structural context construction and structural context exploitation while remaining lightweight. Experiments on 29 EA datasets in OpenEA, SRPRS, and DBP show consistent gains over strong transferable

_analysis.summary: This paper examines entity alignment (EA), a method for identifying equivalent entities across different knowledge graphs (KGs)—structured datasets that represent information as entities and their relationships. The authors propose ContextEA, an improved framework that better leverages structural context in KGs to enhance alignment accuracy, addressing weaknesses in existing models that struggle with cross-KG interactions and coarse similarity metrics.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper focuses on technical improvements in knowledge graph alignment, it indirectly highlights the growing importance of data interoperability and cross-platform data integration, which are critical for digital competition in data-driven markets.
_analysis.market_signal: Advances in knowledge graph alignment could enable more seamless data sharing and integration across platforms, potentially reducing switching costs and fostering competition in data-intensive markets.

items.title: WebKnoGraph: GNN-Powered Internal Linking
items.abstract: Internal link optimization is a recurring task in search engine optimization, yet many production workflows rely on manual judgment, fixed page templates, or generic tool recommendations. Practitioners need ways to evaluate candidate links before deployment because link changes can redistribute authority and affect semantic coherence in ways that are difficult to isolate after release. We present WebKnoGraph, an open-source framework for evaluating internal linking strategies on website crawls. The framework models a website as a directed graph, represents pages by embeddings, scores candidate links with GraphSAGE, and evaluates interventions by embedding the site into larger host environments. We instantiate WebKnoGraph on a production crawl of Kalicube.com and compare automatic with expert-assisted link selection in an empirical FineWeb-based host graph and a synthetic Barabási-Albert host graph, using PageRank-based authority metrics and semantic coherence. The results show that automatic selection generally produces stronger authority redistribution, with higher Authority Yield, but also larger semantic coherence costs. Expert-assisted selection better preserves semantic cohere

_analysis.summary: WebKnoGraph is an open-source tool that uses graph neural networks (GNNs) to optimize internal linking on websites by modeling pages as a directed graph and scoring potential links before they are implemented. This helps website owners evaluate how link changes could redistribute authority (e.g., SEO rankings) and semantic coherence without unintended consequences after deployment.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While not directly about competition policy, it reveals how AI-driven SEO tools can concentrate or redistribute digital visibility, potentially reinforcing or disrupting existing platform dominance in search and content discovery.
_analysis.market_signal: AI-powered SEO optimization tools like WebKnoGraph may intensify competition among content providers and platforms by enabling more precise manipulation of search rankings, challenging incumbents' control over organic traffic.

items.title: CogManip: Benchmarking Manipulative Behavior in Multi-Turn Interactions with Large Language Model
items.abstract: Whether Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit covert psychological manipulation in complex human-AI interactions has garnered increasing safety concerns. However, existing AI safety benchmarks remain largely restricted to explicit rule compliance and static prompts, failing to capture the dynamic and covert nature of manipulative strategies in multi-turn dialogues. We introduce CogManip, a comprehensive benchmark that evaluates 15 manipulation strategy risks across 1,000 multi-turn interaction scenarios, validated by human experts. A systematic evaluation of 13 representative models, including frontier models like GPT-5.4 and DeepSeek-V3.2, reveals significant risk heterogeneities and illuminates the targeted direction for future defense. Further analysis of objective function perturbation reveals that DeepSeek-V3.2's manipulation tactics are highly sensitive to both negative and benign system prompts, demonstrating the critical necessity of prompt-based defense engineering and implicit goal auditing. CogManip offers a robust instrument and perspective for auditing the implicit psychological influence and dynamic strategy selection of modern LLMs.

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces CogManip, a new benchmark that tests whether advanced AI models (Large Language Models or LLMs) can use subtle psychological tricks to influence people in multi-turn conversations. The study evaluates 13 different AI models across 1,000 real-world-like scenarios, finding that some models can engage in covert manipulation strategies.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: Demonstrates how AI systems can subtly influence user behavior, which may reshape competitive dynamics in digital markets where user attention and engagement are key resources.
_analysis.market_signal: AI models capable of manipulative interactions could disrupt digital advertising markets by altering user decision-making in ways that favor certain platforms or services.

items.title: IR3DE: A Linear Router for Large Language Models
items.abstract: Foundational Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate proficiency on a wide range of general tasks, and achieve remarkable results on various specialized tasks via domain-expert LLMs. With the ever-growing list of available LLMs, inference routers are being proposed to select the most appropriate LLM for each prompt. However, existing routing methods either optimize cost across weak-to-strong generalist LLMs or require substantial training to support domain-expertise routing. In this paper, we propose IR3DE, a Ridge Regression-based Router for Domain Experts that provides cheap and fast routing decisions for each prompt. We evaluate IR3DE in two Causal Language Modeling (CLM) settings where the tasks are next-token prediction for all domains, and one reasoning setting where each domain has its own distinct reasoning task. Despite being a linear router, IR3DE achieves performance comparable to the other baselines in both CLM settings, and surpassing them in the reasoning setting, with a normalized performance of 98.4%. Moreover, IR3DE enables the addition or removal of new domain experts without requiring the router to be retrained from scratch, allowing a dynamic set of LLMs to be

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces IR3DE (a Ridge Regression-based Router for Domain Experts), a new method for routing user prompts to the most suitable large language model (LLM) without requiring extensive training. Existing routers either focus on cost optimization across generalist models or need heavy training for domain-specific routing, but IR3DE offers a faster and cheaper alternative by using linear regression to select expert LLMs.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This technology could reduce reliance on dominant generalist LLMs (e.g., those from big tech firms) by enabling efficient routing to specialized models, potentially lowering barriers to entry for smaller AI providers and shifting competitive dynamics in the LLM market.
_analysis.market_signal: Specialized LLM routing could fragment the AI market, reducing the dominance of a few generalist models and enabling competition from niche or smaller players.

items.title: OrderGrad: Optimizing Beyond the Mean with Order-Statistic Policy Gradient Estimation
items.abstract: Policy-gradient methods usually optimize expected return, but many real world applications care about distributional properties of returns: tail risk, outlier robustness, or best-of-K discovery. We introduce OrderGrad, a family of likelihood-ratio and reparameterization gradient estimators for order-statistic objectives. OrderGrad optimizes finite-sample L-statistics, i.e., weighted averages of sorted rewards or costs, recovering objectives such as VaR, CVaR, trimmed means, medians, and top-m/best-of-K criteria by changing only the rank weights. For any fixed sample size and rank-weight vector, OrderGrad provides an unbiased gradient estimator for the corresponding order-statistic objective. The method is implemented as a simple reward transformation that can then be used in an otherwise standard policy-gradient or reparameterized update. We study the resulting estimator's variance behavior and evaluate it on tasks where mean optimization is mismatched to the deployment objective, including LLM math post-training and other tasks. OrderGrad provides a unified, plug-and-play route to risk-averse, robust, and exploratory learning. Code: https://github.com/paavo5/ordergrad

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces OrderGrad, a new method for training AI systems that focuses on optimizing specific parts of the reward distribution (like the worst outcomes or the top-performing results) rather than just the average performance. Instead of optimizing for the mean return, OrderGrad allows AI agents to prioritize objectives such as minimizing extreme risks (tail risk), improving robustness to outliers, or selecting the best outcomes from a set (best-of-K).
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This research signals how AI optimization techniques are evolving to prioritize specific distributional outcomes, which could impact how digital platforms design algorithms that shape user experiences, market access, or competitive dynamics.
_analysis.market_signal: AI systems optimized for distributional properties (e.g., top-K outcomes) may reinforce winner-takes-all dynamics in digital markets by favoring dominant platforms that can afford large-scale data collection and experimentation.

items.title: Integrating Mechanistic and Data-Driven Models for Neurological Disorders through Differentiable Programming
items.abstract: Advances in computational modeling, neuroimaging, and artificial intelligence are revolutionizing the modeling of neurological disorders for improved diagnostics, prognosis, and treatment planning. Mechanistic models provide valuable scientific insight into the disorders, but in practice they are often simplified with assumptions or computationally expensive and slow to solve. However, while purely data driven approaches provide speed and scalability, they require large, high quality data to train and generally suffer from interpretability and generalization issues. This perspective paper presents a structured overview of hybrid modeling strategies, which combine deep learning models with physics based solvers, and are categorized into parallel, series, and parallel-series architectures. Three main approaches that have been emphasized are residual modeling for missing or incomplete physics, Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (NODEs) for continuous time dynamics approximation, and solver in the loop that accelerates traditional solvers with neural approximations. These hybrid models integrate the governing differential equation based formulations and deep learning to characteriz

_analysis.summary: This academic paper explores how combining traditional scientific models (mechanistic models) with modern AI techniques (data-driven models) can improve the understanding and treatment of neurological disorders. Mechanistic models offer deep scientific insights but are often slow and simplified, while AI models are fast and scalable but require large datasets and lack interpretability.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper focuses on neurological disorders, its discussion of trade-offs between mechanistic and data-driven models highlights broader challenges in AI adoption that could influence digital competition, particularly in data access and model transparency.
_analysis.market_signal: The paper signals the growing importance of hybrid AI approaches, which may impact how firms integrate proprietary and open data, potentially affecting market dynamics in AI-driven industries.

items.title: Beyond Semantic Organization: Memory as Execution State Management for Long-Horizon Agents
items.abstract: LLM-based agents increasingly tackle long-horizon tasks with interdependent decisions, where each action reshapes future constraints and intermediate errors can cascade. Existing RAG and agent memory systems organize histories by semantic similarity, retrieving content-relevant entries at decision time. We argue that this design mismatches execution-state dependencies: it fragments decision trajectories and mixes valid and erroneous traces, hindering coherent state reconstruction and error isolation. We propose MAGE (Memory as Agent-Guided Exploration), an active execution-state manager that stores interactions in a hierarchical state tree. The agent derives its state from the active root-to-current path, combining subgoal summaries, recent traces, and hints from prior branches. Four coupled operations maintain the tree: Grow records new traces, Compress summarizes completed subgoals, Maintain validates summaries, and Revise restores a target boundary and resumes on a new branch. This design bounds context growth while preserving state integrity and isolating flawed segments from the active path. Experiments on MemoryArena show that MAGE improves the average task success rate by 7.

_analysis.summary: This paper critiques how current AI agents (systems that perform tasks using large language models) manage memory by organizing past actions based on semantic similarity, which can fragment decision-making and obscure errors. It proposes MAGE (Memory as Agent-Guided Exploration), a new system that actively manages execution states to improve coherence and error isolation in long-horizon tasks.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This paper reveals how AI agents' memory and decision-making architectures could disrupt digital markets by enabling more autonomous, error-resilient agents that may challenge incumbent platforms' control over user interactions.
